entries.title: PopUp Bagels’ will pop open Friday in Lincoln Park
entries.summary: <p>Known for its “grip, rip and dip” method, PopUp Bagel’s has five bagel flavors consumers can dip in heaping containers of various schmears.</p><p>PopUp Bagels has been opening locations across the United States, and now Chicagoans will get the chance see what all the fuss is about.</p><p>The grand opening of its first Chicago location, in Lincoln Park, is at 7 a.m. Friday. The shop, at 2321 N. Lincoln Ave., will feature a limited edition "Giardiniera Schmear" in partnership with Portillo's Hot Dogs. </p><p>“Our grip, rip, and dip model is designed for batch enjoyment and flexibility, allowing guests to enjoy bagels how they want, where they want, and with whoever they want,” PopUp Bagels’ website says.</p><p>PopUp offers plain, everything, poppyseed, salt or sesame bagels and different cream cheese or butter schmears for dipping. Besides plain cream cheese, there are flavored options, such as cake batter cream cheese or vanilla berry butter. Customers also can expect limited-time special schmears weekly.</p><p>While the company’s slogan is “not famous but known,” PopUp Bagels has had its viral moments. After opening its first location in Westport, Connecticut during the pandemic, PopUp gained popularity over social media nearly one year later.</p><p>With long lines often snaking along the street outside of its shops, PopUps are nearly impossible to miss.</p><p>“Hot bagels are baked continuously throughout the day to meet demand,” the website says. “Trust us, the ‘wait’ is part of the experience.”</p><p>The Chicago shop will be open from 7 a.m. to 4 p.m., seven days a week. Catering is available.<br></p>
entries.author: Zoe Singer

entries.title: An early look at 15 teams to keep an eye on ahead of the 2026-27 boys basketball season
entries.summary: <p>State championships were won several weeks ago. Marist broke its glass ceiling with a trip to Champaign and a Class 4A title, while Marshall restored its past glory with a Class 1A state championship. Now it’s on to 2026-27 and a look ahead to 15 of the area’s top teams next season.</p><p><b>Benet</b></p><p>The program has churned out a high level of talent the last two years, including four Division I prospects, while winning 69 games and playing for two state titles. Ed Stasys is next. The 6-8 junior will be joined by 6-5 junior Perry Tchiegne and more talented young players in the pipeline.</p><p><b>Bolingbrook</b></p><p>When the starting point is Brady Pettigrew, one of the top prospects in the country in the Class of 2028, you’re in pretty good shape.</p><p></p><p><b>Evanston</b></p><p>The Wildkits will be loaded with a return of four starters from a team that won 27 games, including Central Suburban League South Player of the Year Vito Rocca.</p><p><b>Glenbard North</b></p><p>The follow-up to a 24-win team that won conference and regional championships could be even better, thanks to the return of 6-4 Lamari Carpenter and 6-5 Oturo Redento.</p><p><b>Homewood-Floss.</b></p><p>Sophomore Darrius Hawkins Jr., one of the top point guards in the state, was the catalyst for a 29-win team. Another sophomore, 6-6 John Brown IV, and junior Danny Ruffin will take on bigger roles.</p><p><b>Kenwood</b></p><p>The Public League is there for the taking next season, so the returning combination of Osiris Bell and Damari Stephens give the Broncos an early edge.</p><p><b>Leo</b></p><p>Led by Brian Kizer, all five starters return from a team that finished fourth in Class 3A. Plus, promising 6-8 freshman Elon Henderson should make a considerable jump.</p><p><b>Lockport</b></p><p>The<br>Porters, who won 27 games and a regional championship, should be even better with the return of twins Nedas and Nojus Venckus.</p><p><b>Lyons</b></p><p>The Lions will be bolstered by the return of three key players with size and scoring in 6-6 sophomore Grant Smith and a pair of juniors, 6-5 Timmy Sloan and 6-5 Nate Woods.</p><p><b>Mount Carmel</b></p><p>The Caravan have a talented trio to build around: the freshman pair of DK Heard and Ronald Johnson, who both opened eyes last season, and versatile junior Logan Wessel.</p><p><b>Neuqua Valley</b></p><p>There isn’t a better 1-2 punch in the state returning than 6-5 junior and Illinois commit Mason Martin and 6-6 sophomore star Cole Kelly, one of the top players nationally in the Class of 2028. With a more experienced supporting cast, might the Wildcats be the preseason No. 1?</p><p><b>Oswego</b></p><p>Sophomore Ethan Vahl is a dynamic scorer in the backcourt, while 6-10 sophomore Cole Jansons is a fast-improving big man. Stat-sheet-stuffing Graham Schwab also returns for a team that won 22 games.</p><p><b>St. Ignatius</b></p><p>The loss of Chris Bolte will be felt, but blossoming sophomore guards Amir Tucker and DJ Caldwell will keep the Wolfpack relevant.</p><p></p><p><b>St. Laurence</b></p><p>There is a surplus of young talent ready to take the Vikings to another level. Rugged guard Markese Peoples had a breakout junior year, while junior Reggie Stevens and sophomores Noah Corro, Jeremiah Toney and Logan Brown are all back.</p><p><b>Warren</b></p><p>The state’s best player, junior guard Jaxson Davis, will keep the Blue Devils contending at a high level.</p>
entries.author: Joe Henricksen

entries.title: FAA orders summer flight reduction at O'Hare, citing safety, efficiency
entries.summary: <p>Citing safety and efficiency concerns, the Federal Aviation Administration announced Thursday it will cut the daily flight volume at O’Hare International Airport this summer by more than 300 flights.</p><p>The reduction comes as airlines had planned an increase in flights for this summer compared to last year. That includes the airport’s two largest airlines, United Airlines and American Airlines, which have been battling for gate space at O’Hare, the country’s busiest airport.</p><p>The FAA will limit O’Hare’s daily flights to 2,708 between from May 17 through Oct. 24.</p><p>That is down from the 3,080 daily flights that airlines had planned for this summer.</p><p>U.S. Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy said the original summer schedule was “unrealistic” and would have “dramatically” exceeded what the airport’s infrastructure and air traffic controllers could handle. The initial summer 2026 schedule would have been a 15% peak-day increase from last summer, the FAA said.</p><p>“Our number one priority is the safety of the flying public, and that means ensuring airline schedules reflect what the system can safely handle,” FAA Administrator Bryan Bedford said in a statement.</p><p>Less than 60% of flights at O'Hare were on time last summer, according to the FAA.</p><p>The FAA worked with the airlines and officials at O’Hare to reach a compromise between reducing operations and meeting the airlines’ needs.</p><p>United Airlines said the reduction is a “solution that makes sense for everyone who cares about O’Hare’s success.”</p><p>A spokesperson for American Airlines said the company is “pleased to have secured a sufficient level of flights through the FAA’s process to operate a successful hub at O’Hare this summer.”</p><p>“We are grateful to Secretary Duffy, Administrator Bedford, and their leadership teams for acting swiftly to ensure that Chicagoans and all consumers continue to benefit from sensible competition and to help minimize flight disruptions during the busy summer season,” American Airlines said.</p><p>A spokesperson for the Chicago Department of Aviation called the FAA’s order a “thoughtful approach” to ensure the limitations do not extend past the summer and that flight operations aren’t less than last year.</p><p>Last year, the FAA targeted O’Hare for flight cuts during a government shutdown. It led to controller shortages and dozens of daily flight cancellations.</p>
entries.author: Kade Heather

entries.title: White Sox head west with that familiar losing feeling
entries.summary: <p>Swept at home and hitting the road for six games out West with a 6-13 record in tow is no way to start a season, but that’s where the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" >White Sox</a> found themselves Thursday after a stinging 5-3 loss to the Rays before an announced crowd of 10,128 at Rate Field.</p><p>As they embark on road series against the Athletics and Diamondbacks, both teams with winning records, the Sox sport the worst record in the majors after losing eight of their last nine games and six in a row at home. They are on an early trajectory to 111 losses, which would mark their fourth consecutive season of 100 losses or more. The Phillies lost 100 or more for five consecutive seasons, from 1938-42. Three other teams have lost 100 or more in four straight years, the 1909-12 Boston Braves, 1962-65 Mets and 1961-64 Washington Senators.</p><p>Perhaps it’s too soon to dig up such history, but the general consensus around these parts entering the season was that a corner would be turned with the current rebuild. But a .316 win percentage is doing nothing to support that suggestion. The Sox have pitched well enough, but ranking third to last in the majors in runs scored and going 5-for-39 (.128) with runners in scoring position the last seven games isn’t cutting it. There are days like Thursday when hard-hit balls don’t find safe places to land — manager Will Venable noted it has been like that for the last four games — but so it goes in baseball, which doesn’t cut slack for light-hitting teams.</p><p>“Still just missing that big hit and putting some crooked numbers up there,” said Venable, who watched Miguel Vargas and Everson Pereira homer and catcher Edgar Quero double for only his first RBI. “Have to keep going.”</p><p>“Yeah, we are frustrated,” Quero said.</p><p>Munetaka Murakami, the Japanese home-run threat signed in the offseason, was frustrated with an 0-for-4 with his 24th, 25th and 26th strikeouts after flying out to deep center in the first. Murakami (.167) has five homers but only 10 hits, the others singles to go with a hefty 17 walks.</p><p>The book on Murakami was his vulnerability to high heat, but he has been whiffing on off-speed stuff as well.</p><p>“There are more base hits in there,” Sox director of hitting Ryan Fuller promised. “You saw it in Japan. His ability with two strikes to have a little bit of a trade-off. It might not be the home-run swing but dial it back a little bit, see the ball a little bit deeper. Use the whole field.”</p><p>The Sox’ other notable free-agent signee, closer Seranthony Dominguez, failed to protect the lead handed to him with Pereira’s homer in the eighth, giving up a tying homer to Junior Caminero, then putting the fourth and fifth runs on base via a walk and hit batsman before exiting to a smattering of boos.</p><p>“I don’t know, they just found a way to keep touching the ball every time, every single pitch,” Dominguez said. “They just did a great job keeping alive.</p><p>“Unfortunately I didn’t do my job today, but I have to do it next time.”</p><p>Lucas Sims replaced Dominguez with the score tied, walking in the Rays fourth and fifth runs.</p><p>It has been that kind of stretch for the Sox, who are not avoiding the avoidable mistakes that only exacerbate things. On Wednesday, Murakami wandered off base thinking it was strike three and got picked off. On Thursday, Derek Hill singled Tanner Murray to third base with two outs in the second but got caught by the cutoff man in an inning-ending rundown.</p><p>“Just not the right decision on that ball,” Venable said. “Once he was in the rundown, especially when [first baseman Ryan] Vilade got the ball at that point, Tanner has to go ahead and try to take home.”</p><p>“It’s tough,” Pereira said. “You don’t want to have any losses, especially not like this one. But it’s part of the game, too, you just have to keep going.”</p><p>We shall see where it goes.</p><p></p><p></p><div class="PageListL" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" entries.author: Daryl Van Schouwen

entries.title: Friends, family honor Hyde Park Academy student killed in bus stop shooting — 'He was a baby'
entries.summary: <p>Ali Malone was among those releasing silver, black and blue balloons Thursday afternoon outside the South Side YMCA, yelling "We love you, king."</p><p>It was in honor of her cousin Eric Billups, 16, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/04/15/two-teens-shot-bus-stop-woodlawn-stony-island" >who was fatally shot Wednesday</a> — at the same place and about the same time that she and others gathered a day later to mourn him. A Hyde Park Academy High School classmate also was wounded. </p><p>"He was a baby, he didn't deserve this," Malone said. "It's senseless, it's stupid and something has to change."</p><p>Jerri Billups, Eric's grandmother, also was there Thursday afternoon. She said Eric was an avid football and basketball player who could answer any question she threw his way. He "kept her knowing" with his smarts, she said. Now she and other family members looking for answers to questions they didn't expect to ask.</p><p>"He wasn't into nothing, I don't understand why [he was shot] five times. Why?"</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-f50000" name="image-f50000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4054c49/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5233x2937+0+276/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F5f%2Fcb%2Fd5870a4b4748860f315a6aee06f2%2Fteenshotfolo-260417-001-jpeg.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/201a9ac/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5233x2937+0+276/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F5f%2Fcb%2Fd5870a4b4748860f315a6aee06f2%2Fteenshotfolo-260417-001-jpeg.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/67c8532/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5233x2937+0+276/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F5f%2Fcb%2Fd5870a4b4748860f315a6aee06f2%2Fteenshotfolo-260417-001-jpeg.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Ali Malone, a cousin of Eric Billups, writes a message for Eric on a poster on Thursday, April 16, 2026, at the bus stop outside the South Side YMCA in Hyde Park. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/67c8532/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5233x2937+0+276/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F5f%2Fcb%2Fd5870a4b4748860f315a6aee06f2%2Fteenshotfolo-260417-001-jpeg.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6ac171d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5233x2937+0+276/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F5f%2Fcb%2Fd5870a4b4748860f315a6aee06f2%2Fteenshotfolo-260417-001-jpeg.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/67c8532/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5233x2937+0+276/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F5f%2Fcb%2Fd5870a4b4748860f315a6aee06f2%2Fteenshotfolo-260417-001-jpeg.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Ali Malone, a cousin of Eric Billups, writes a message for Eric on a poster on Thursday at the bus stop outside the South Side YMCA in Hyde Park. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anastasia Busby/For the Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Police statements and CPD reports obtained by the Sun-Times outline what police believe happened.</p><p>The shooter and Billups had made plans to fight each other after school Wednesday, witnesses told police. After school was dismissed, Eric and a 16-year-old girl went to a bus stop in the 6300 block of South Stony Island Avenue, about a block from Hyde Park Academy. Around 3:25 p.m., the suspect walked up to him, pulled a handgun out of his backpack and opened fire.</p><p>It was unclear what the dispute was about, but the alleged shooter, who had been released from school early, returned to the area still wearing a Hyde Park Academy uniform, according to the police reports.</p><p>Eric was shot at least five times and was unresponsive at the scene, officers said. He was rushed to the University of Chicago Medical Center in critical condition and pronounced dead at 3:48 p.m., according to the Cook County medical examiner's office.</p><p>Police found 12 shell 9mm casings and two book bags at the scene, the report said. No arrests had been made as of Thursday afternoon.</p><p>The girl, also 16, was taken to the University of Chicago Medical Center, where she was listed in fair condition after being shot in her calf.</p><p>Eric's mom was too distraught to speak when reached by a Sun-Times reporter.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-7e0000" name="image-7e0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f530938/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4957x2782+0+262/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F34%2F00%2F6d3fa3cb4fce9967b37a2e85aacb%2Fteenshotfolo-260417-010-jpeg.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7e95b29/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4957x2782+0+262/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F34%2F00%2F6d3fa3cb4fce9967b37a2e85aacb%2Fteenshotfolo-260417-010-jpeg.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/93f9462/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4957x2782+0+262/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F34%2F00%2F6d3fa3cb4fce9967b37a2e85aacb%2Fteenshotfolo-260417-010-jpeg.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Eric Billups’ mom, Rolanda Phoenix (left), greets family and friends before the balloon release to honor her son on Thursday at the bus stop outside the South Side YMCA in Hyde Park.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anastasia Busby/For the Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>In a letter sent to the Hyde Park Academy community, Principal Rosette Edinburg noted "some members of our school community were impacted by this incident" and the school was working to offer support services through the CPS Crisis Management Unit and its own school-based mental health professionals. </p><p>"We are extremely saddened by this incident and will do everything we can to help our school community heal," the letter states.</p><p>“All of us at Chicago Public Schools are saddened to learn of the loss of a young person’s life, and we extend our heartfelt condolences to the family, friends, school and community members impacted by this tragedy,” CPS said in a statement. </p><p>Billups used to drive her grandson to the Kenwood-based high school from their West Side home when he was a freshman, but stopped as he got older. </p><p>She worried about his safety, and continues to worry about other students. She said she wants to see security outside the school now.</p><p>"I didn't want him on the bus," Billups said. "Now this happened. ... You don't know until it hits your house, and it hit me pretty hard. He's well missed."</p><p>Ald. Desmon Yancy (5th) said Thursday morning he was "still processing" the shooting in his ward.</p><p>Yancy noted it's been a "rough few weeks" for the Hyde Park Academy community. <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/22/girl-15-killed-while-riding-scooter-in-south-shore-hit-and-run" target="_blank" >Violet Harris</a>, a 15-year-old student, was killed after a vehicle crashed into the electric scooter she was riding last month in South Shore and <a class="Link" href="https://abc7chicago.com/post/family-mourning-hyde-park-high-school-student-killed-dolton-hit-run/18828745/" target="_blank" >Lania Smith</a>, an 18-year-old senior, was killed in a hit-and-run in Dolton earlier this month.</p><p>"It's still difficult to understand how young people are dealing with trauma and grief while still having easy access to handguns," Yancy said. "[But] it's better for us to try and find ways to support each other through this moment and at the same time figure out what role you can play in impacting young people so that this violence doesn’t become an option for them."</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-aa0000" entries.title: Horoscope for Friday, April 17, 2026
entries.summary: <h2>Moon Alert</h2> <p>Avoid shopping (except food and gas) and important decisions from 10 a.m. to noon. After that, the moon moves from Aries into Taurus. (The New Moon peaks in Aries at 6:52 a.m.)</p><h2>Aries (March 21-April 19)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> This might be the best day of the year to take a realistic look in the mirror and ask yourself what you can do to improve your image and the impression you make on your world. Hairstyle? Wardrobe? Shoes? Ideas?</p><h2>Taurus (April 20-May 20)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Today's new moon is the perfect time to contemplate your navel because you have lots to think about. You are now in a two-year window where you will let go of what is no longer relevant in your life — people, places and possessions. (And you're the collector of the zodiac.)</p><h2>Gemini (May 21-June 20)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Each month, the new moon is an opportunity to make resolutions. Today is the perfect time for you to think about friends as well as groups and organizations to which you might belong. You want to be with people who inspire and benefit you.</p><h2>Cancer (June 21-July 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> If you don’t have a goal for where you’re headed, others will make one for you. Life is like that because, as we all know, nature abhors a vacuum. This is a good day to think about where you want to be five years from now. What’s the plan, Stan?</p><h2>Leo (July 23-Aug. 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; (This is in your own best interest because your own happiness matters.) For a relationship to be successful, you must be as good for your partner as he or she is for you.</p><h2>Scorpio (Oct. 23-Nov. 21)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> You're strong-willed and resourceful, which is why you'll be glad to know that today is the best day of the year to make resolutions about how to stay healthy and improve your overall well-being. It's also the perfect day to think about how to work smarter.</p><h2>Sagittarius (Nov. 22-Dec. 21)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-6f0000" name="html-embed-module-6f0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>dynamic</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> More than most signs, you are future-oriented. You’re always wondering what’s around the next corner. Today’s new moon is an excellent day for you to think about how to use your creative talents. It’s also the perfect day to plan a future vacation.</p><h2>Capricorn (Dec. 22-Jan. 19)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; From the word springs the deed. The deed soon becomes habit, and habit eventually hardens into character." Therefore, we must watch our thoughts! Today's new moon urges you to observe your daily thoughts — the future you.</p><h2>Pisces (Feb. 19-March 20)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> This is an excellent day to think about your values — in other words, what really matters to you in life? If you don't know what really matters, what will guide you when you have to make important decisions?</p><h2>If today is your birthday</h2><p>Actor Sean Bean (1959) shares your birthday today. You're organized, methodical and ambitious. You have a strong sense of natural justice and a desire to champion the downtrodden. This is a year of building and construction. Create foundations both internally and externally. Simplicity is key. Take charge of your health. Stay grounded and levelheaded.</p>
entries.author: Georgia Nicols

entries.title: 3 hurt when SUV hits cyclist, fire hydrant, fence in Gage Park neighborhood
entries.summary: <p>Three people were hurt when an SUV rear-ended a cyclist before hitting a fire hydrant and a fence around a car dealership late Thursday on the Southwest Side. </p><p>The crash happened around 11:40 p.m. in the 5800 block of South Western Avenue when a 22-year-old cyclist was struck from behind by a black SUV, Chicago police said. </p><p>The driver then struck a fire hydrant and struck the fence of a car dealership, causing the SUV to roll over, police said. </p><p>The 25-year-old driver was taken to Mount Sinai Hospital where his condition wasn’t immediately known, officials said. A male passenger in the SUV was taken to the same hospital with minor injuries and was in fair condition. </p><p>The cyclist suffered cuts to left arm and trauma to his body and was also taken to Mount Sinai where he was in fair condition, police said. </p><p>The Major Accidents Investigation Unit was investigating.</p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Takeaways from Blackhawks exit interviews: Summer training schedules could overlap in Chicago
entries.summary: <p>Exit interviews Thursday marked the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks" target="_blank" >Blackhawks</a>' last official event of the season, so every player is free to go wherever they want starting Friday. For most, that means going home.</p><p>But the team expects to still see a lot of each other this summer — more than in years past. For one thing, a bunch of Hawks are planning to spend at least part of the summer training in Chicago.</p><p>"It's been me and [Connor] Murphy the last couple summers [in Chicago], just us two," Alex Vlasic said. "I'm really pumped to get some more guys in here. It builds good team camaraderie. We're all going to have a good time training together. It pushes each other."</p><p>There's also a golf trip — starting right away — that a handful of guys, including Ryan Greene, are going on. And <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/04/14/blackhawks-oliver-moore-studying-nhl-sharpening-brain-summer" target="_blank" >Oliver Moore</a> and <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/04/16/connor-bedard-blackhawks-captain-ready-leadership-alex-vlasic" target="_blank" >Connor Bedard</a> are taking a trip to Florida. The calendar is already crowded.</p><p>"I’ll be like, 'You've got to come to [Vancouver],'" Bedard said. "And then someone from Toronto will be like, 'You've got to come to Toronto.' It’s like we've got 10 trips lined up to see everyone. I don’t know if we’ll make all of them.</p><p>"It’s a special group. I don’t think there was a day this year where just left the rink and chilled alone. Guys would always be getting together. Me and Moore live in the same building, so I think he’s got an imprint on my couch for the all the time he’s spent there."</p><h3><b>Bound for Worlds</b></h3><p>Moore is the only Hawk confirmed so far for the upcoming world championships. He will play for Team USA in the tournament May 15-31 in Switzerland.</p><p>When Frank Nazar did that last year — in a similar position as Moore, coming off his first NHL season — he led the team in scoring and led them to a gold medal. It substantially elevated his status. The best-case scenario for Moore would be following in Nazar's footsteps.</p><p>Swedish media reported Anton Frondell will be selected for their team, too, but Frondell said he didn't know yet.</p><h3><b>Levshunov’s outlook</b></h3><p>There's no mystery about what coach Jeff Blashill and enigmatic defenseman Artyom Levshunov discussed in their exit meeting.</p><p>Said Blashill: "He’s almost a freak athlete, body-wise. He’s really strong, really powerful. I think there’s another level of learning to push [yourself] and not pace himself. Things like that that can be done through summer training and learning how to just push yourself to the max at all times."</p><p>Said Levshunov: "The coach said, 'Push myself to maximum every shift. I shouldn't be afraid to [get] tired. Push myself to maximum full game, every shift, every period.' That's important aspect for my improvement."</p><p>The Belarusian will travel back and forth between Chicago and Florida, where he works his longtime trainer <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2025/09/10/blackhawks-artyom-levshunov-nhl-rookie-camp-kirill-metlyuk-trainer" target="_blank" >Kirill Metlyuk</a>, throughout this summer. </p><p>In about two weeks, he should be recovered from the fracture in his left hand, which ended his season March 28 and which he still wore a brace on Thursday.</p><p>Blashill mentioned wanting Levshunov to also work on improving his shooting technique (something the Hawks have been <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/01/29/blackhawks-artyom-levshunov-shooting-technique-velocity-deception" target="_blank" >working on</a> since January) and his hip flexibility (which affects his skating motion).</p> <p>"[Arty has] a choice to make: He can make it a great summer, a good summer or an OK summer," Blashill said. "If he makes it either of the last two, it’s not good enough. We need a great summer out of him, just like everybody else."</p><h3><b>Mikheyev’s limbo</b></h3><p>General manager Kyle Davidson said he still hopes to re-sign Ilya Mikheyev, a pending unrestricted free agent, but there haven't been substantial talks since the trade deadline.</p><p>"His motor and his compete and drive, it’s all something I want to be and a player I want to be like, for sure," Oliver Moore said. "Anytime you can have Ilya Mikheyev on your team, your team’s going to be better. He was our best penalty killer all season. I don’t know how many stick-on-pucks he had, but it was ridiculous."</p><p>Mikheyev's presence would have the added bonus of giving Levshunov and incoming Russian prospect Roman Kantserov a friend who can speak their native language.</p><p>"I like him as a player, like him as a human," Levshunov said. "I can't say that I wouldn't like to see him. But it's not my decision. It's his decision, and the organization's."</p><h3><b>Knight’s musings</b></h3><p>Spencer Knight made 55 starts this season, tied for seventh-most among NHL goalies, after making 36 last season. Aside from few illnesses during the second half, he felt like his body handled the increased workload well.</p><p>"I feel like I could've played more," Knight said. "And I'm going to have to play more at some point, right? If you play 50-60 [games] in the regular season, you're going to have to play 70-80 if you play playoffs, which is what we're going to try to do at some point."</p><p>When he's in a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2025/11/30/blackhawks-nhl-goalie-spencer-knight-off-ice-habits-red-light-mat" target="_blank" >philosophical mood</a>, his musings can be fascinating, which was the case during a 13-minute interview Thursday.</p><p>"It doesn't get easier, you know?" he said. "Once you make the NHL, OK. You're backup, all right. Then you establish yourself as an NHL-er, and become a starter, and become a good starter and a great starter. Then you've got to be someone who can succeed. Even if you're good, if the team doesn't have success, it might wear on you. </p><p>"It's always going to get harder, so I'm always preparing for more. But I do feel like doing it is nice, knowing I have a little more experience in that department. It'll help me going forward."</p><h3><b>Notes</b></h3><p>Davidson mentioned Vaclav Nestrasil, the 25th overall pick in last year's draft, as a prospect who especially impressed him this season. Nestrasil racked up 31 points in 34 games as a freshman at UMass.</p><p>The Panthers finished eighth-to-last in the NHL standings due to the Blues and Sharks' wins Thursday. That means the Panthers' first-round draft pick is guaranteed to fall in the top 10 and won't be transferred to the Hawks, who will instead receive the Panthers' unprotected 2027 first-round pick.</p>
entries.author: Ben Pope

entries.title: CPS students need more time in classroom, not May Day off
entries.summary: <p>The Chicago Teachers Union's <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2026/03/12/chicago-teachers-union-plans-to-join-may-1-no-school-no-work-day-of-action-wants-classes-canceled" >push to treat May 1</a> as a day tied to political activity is being framed as a stand for workers’ rights. But for many Chicago Public Schools parents, the reality looks very different: an added burden of taking off work and finding a sitter for their kids. What’s presented as activism ultimately functions as a three-day weekend for most staff, with no meaningful systemwide disruption and no clear educational benefit for students.</p><p>Meanwhile, the instructional time lost is simply shifted, not recovered, raising legitimate concerns about priorities in a district already struggling with academics, truancy, absentee rates and dwindling enrollment.</p><p>If this arrangement results in a paid day off followed by a low-impact “makeup” day later in June, it’s fair for parents and taxpayers to question whether students are being put first. This switch ensures that a real day of classroom instruction is replaced with a late-year “do-nothing” day, when no students are present and staff are more likely left to self-directed time while watching Netflix on their laptops rather than meaningful teaching on May 1.</p><p>CPS should be focused on maximizing classroom time and stability, not enabling schedule changes that appear to benefit the CTU's political agenda more than students. Transparency and accountability, not political symbolism forced onto students by the CTU, should guide decisions that affect over 300,000 students.</p><p><i>Walter Brzeski, Dunning</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-9c0000" name="module-9c0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>Give us your take</h3><br>Send letters to the editor to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. To be considered for publication, letters must include your full name, your neighborhood or hometown and a phone number for verification purposes. Letters should be a maximum of approximately 375 words.</div> </div><p></p><h3>Sox, Hawks and Bulls letting fans down</h3><p>I think that Chicago has the best sports fans in the country. Why is it that many of our sports teams don’t reciprocate with good-performing teams? We currently have three teams — the White Sox, Blackhawks and Bulls — that can be described as poor to mediocre. Management claims it's a rebuilding season. But every season? The Blackhawks should follow the Bulls lead and clean out the front office. These teams should get people with the experience and success to guide these teams to the playoffs.</p><p>There are still a few rough edges with the Cubs and the Bears that need to be smoothed out. But they are headed in the right direction with postseason play. I feel that they’re at least giving back to their loyal fans.</p><p>Considering what these teams take in at the turnstiles, they should use that money to invest in experienced people in the front office so we can have more wins than losses.</p><p><i>Thomas Norlock, Darien</i></p><h3>A word to the wise</h3><p>Alden Loury's lamentable column headlined, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/columnists/2026/04/11/black-segregation-racism-economic-mobility-chicago-opportunity-insights-alden-loury" >“Segregation, racism stifle economic mobility for many in the Black community,” </a>is painfully true regarding Chicago neighborhoods and schools. Black youths need to heed a <a class="Link" href="welcome2thebronx.com/2014/08/5-years-ago-today-sonia-sotomayor-became-first-latina-supreme-court-justice/" target="_blank" >statement</a> by U.S. Supreme Court Justice Sonia Sotomayor: "I would warn any minority student today against the temptations of self-segregation: Take support and comfort from your own group as you can, but don't hide within it." That's good advice!</p><p><i>Jesse Prince, retired circuit judge, Orland Park</i><br></p>
entries.author: Letters to the Editor

entries.title: Dear Abby: Man's online shopping drains coffers and wife's patience
entries.summary: <p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> My husband of 50 years sits in his chair for hours at a time making online purchases he thinks are a necessity or just plain intriguing. We get two or three packages a day, which usually consist of "Buy two and get a third one free" — fruit corers, citrus squeezers, air fresheners, car-washing supplies, cellphone holders, etc.</p><p>What's frustrating is that we already have the perfectly functional items he's purchased. Most of the time they get pushed aside and not used. He also buys food items that are on a repeat subscription, filling our pantry with so much overflow that we must throw away perfectly good food to make room for the newest items.</p><p>These purchases go on his credit card and amount to more than $2,000 per month. My career supported us financially, so my husband didn't have to work, but he was busy with the family in many other ways. Now that I'm retired and no longer have an income, I look at the financial drain his compulsive online shopping has been causing.</p><p>We cordially and sometimes humorously discuss his obsession, and he promises to abstain, but the next week, 15 more unnecessary items are delivered. What can I do to avoid being buried under junk in my own home? — <i>COVERED IN IT IN CALIFORNIA</i></p><p><b>DEAR COVERED:</b> You have identified what your problem is: compulsive spending. Now that you are retired, I'm sure the money your husband has been blowing could be put to better use. For some people, compulsive shopping and overspending can become an addiction. Your husband may be one of those; others find the dopamine rush helps with depression.</p><p>The next time you and your husband discuss his online activities, tell him a 12-step organization called Spenders Anonymous helps people overcome this problem. You can learn more online at spenders.org.</p><p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> I have a friend, "Maron," who is 65 and divorced from her husband, who had been extremely verbally abusive. Her adult son has a daughter by a former partner who had no interest in the girl. The girl, "Lizzie," is living with her father (Maron's son) in his father's home.</p><p>Maron has taken an active interest in her grandchild and spends a lot of time with her. Maron drives Lizzie to preschool even though it is an hour away from her and only a few minutes from her ex-husband's home.</p><p>Recently, Maron's ex has been pressuring her to move back in with him. He says Lizzie needs a consistent female presence. However, he also accuses her of being selfish, thus perpetuating his verbal abuse. What should she do? — <i>IN THE MIDDLE IN WASHINGTON</i></p><p><b>DEAR IN THE MIDDLE:</b> Unless Maron wants to escape the fire only to jump back into the frying pan, she should maintain her separate residence. Because her ex's verbal abuse was so bad she left the marriage, she should not allow herself to be guilted into allowing more of it.</p><p><i>Dear Abby is written by Abigail Van Buren, also known as Jeanne Phillips, and was founded by her mother, Pauline Phillips. Contact Dear Abby at www.DearAbby.com or P.O. Box 69440, Los Angeles, CA 90069.</i></p><p>What teens need to know about sex, drugs, AIDS and getting along with peers and parents is in "What Every Teen Should Know." Send your name and mailing address, plus check or money order for $8 (U.S. funds) to: Dear Abby, Teen Booklet, P.O. Box 446, Kings Mills, OH 45034-0446. (Shipping and handling are included in the price.)</p>
entries.author: Abigail Van Buren

entries.title: Future of food in Illinois extends beyond agriculture
entries.summary: <p>Illinois is home to some of the most fertile farmland in the world, yet <a class="Link" href="https://extension.illinois.edu/news-releases/what-needed-create-long-term-sustainable-food-system-illinois" target="_blank" >almost 95%</a> of the food Illinoisans consume comes from outside the state. Even more troubling? More than <a class="Link" href="https://www.feedingamerica.org/hunger-in-america/illinois" target="_blank" >1.6 million residents</a> in Illinois experience hunger — and nearly 420,000 of them are children.</p><p>As families face rising grocery prices and supply shocks — from avian flu outbreaks disrupting poultry production to climbing beef prices — the need for a more reliable food system has never been more urgent. And as parts of our state grapple with ongoing drought, it’s becoming clear that the future of food must also be climate-resilient and less dependent on scarce natural resources. That’s why Illinois has started taking serious steps toward strengthening our food security and investing in a system that actually serves the people who live here.</p><p>Two years ago, my colleagues and I took a bold step by creating the <a class="Link" href="https://govappointments.illinois.gov/boardsandcommissions/details/?id=fec71beb-163d-ee11-bdf4-001dd80974af" target="_blank" >Illinois Alternative Protein Innovation Task Force</a> — the first of its kind in the nation — to answer a simple but forward-looking question: How can our state lead in the next chapter of food production?</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-f60000" name="module-f60000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Commentary bug</div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h2>Commentary</h2></div> </div><p>Alternative proteins — foods made from plants, cultivated from animal cells, or fermentation-derived — give us the chance to produce more of what we eat right here at home. They use fewer resources, are more resilient and offer families additional choices at the grocery store. Having local food on store shelves increases stability when prices spike or supplies falter.</p><p>It’s also a major economic opportunity. Illinois is a hub for this growing industry, with companies choosing to headquarter in our state because of our workforce, agriculture industry and top-tier research institutions.</p><p>Illinois ranks third in the nation, behind only California and New York, for the number of companies making alternative protein products. Global leaders like ADM are expanding operations in Decatur. <a class="Link" href="https://www.axios.com/local/chicago/2024/07/31/natures-fynd-plant-based-protein" target="_blank" >Nature’s Fynd</a>, based in Chicago’s historic Union Stockyards, is making breakfast patties using microbes discovered in Yellowstone hot springs. Lincoln Park’s <a class="Link" href="https://clevercarnivore.com/" target="_blank" >Clever Carnivore</a> is focused on bringing cultivated meatball and bratwurst prototypes to market. And the <a class="Link" href="https://ifabtechhub.org/" target="_blank" >University of Illinois’ iFAB Tech Hub</a>, supported through state and federal investment, is turning Illinois crops like corn and soybean into the building blocks of sustainable food and other bio-based products — catalyzing Illinois’ biomanufacturing industry and creating jobs statewide.</p><p>Students in Illinois are also invested. At the University of Illinois and University of Chicago, a growing community of students is helping drive this momentum forward through the Good Food Institute’s <a class="Link" href="https://gfi.org/the-alt-protein-project/" target="_blank" >Alt Protein Project,</a> an international student movement advancing research and innovation in this growing field. Students are also gaining practical experience at <a class="Link" href="https://ibrl.aces.illinois.edu/" target="_blank" >U. of I. Urbana-Champaign’s Integrated Bioprocessing Research Laboratory</a>, a dynamic hub connecting industry and academia to deliver real-world training through paid internships and research opportunities. </p><p>Together, these programs are preparing the next generation of scientists, engineers and entrepreneurs for careers that barely existed a decade ago — a clear sign that Illinois’ future leaders are already preparing to carry this work forward.</p><p>This work isn’t about replacing traditional agriculture; it’s about expanding it. Illinois farmers already grow the corn, soy and wheat that power both animal-based and alternative proteins. By diversifying what we produce and how we produce it, we can open new markets for these crops while supporting the next generation of farmers, scientists and manufacturers.</p><p>That’s why I’m extremely proud of the different perspectives this task force brought together. Researchers, farmers, nonprofits, food manufacturers and state officials all joined forces with the shared goal of strengthening Illinois’ economic and agricultural future. Together, we identified practical steps the state can take, like investing in university research and development, building workforce programs and increasing local food production to bolster the alternative protein industry in Illinois.</p><p>We have a long history of leading in food and agriculture. This work continues that tradition. When we work together, we can solve big problems in ways that benefit communities across the state — from Chicago to Carbondale.</p><p>The task force has given us a road map. Now it’s up to us to turn these recommendations into action. By investing in the future of food, we are investing in the future of Illinois — its workers, its farmers and its families.</p><p><i>State Sen. Mattie Hunter represents the 3rd District and serves as assistant majority leader in the Illinois Senate.</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-3f0000" name="module-3f0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody">Send letters to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. More about <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/submitting-opinion-and-letters" >how to submit here</a>.</div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: State Sen. Mattie Hunter

entries.title: I grew up on Zoom and social media. Protect Illinois kids, teens from the dangers of AI
entries.summary: <p>My generation didn't get a grace period. We were the kids <a class="Link" href="https://urldefense.com/v3/__https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK619280/__;!!KZXpRQ!QyuBQtA6hjtGb1hZbMhjAUvGF565co__MpN3Mt4v8NRU88igHEJqpR6NivE2xraGrOvPXGla5IaDXl1aFiBgkIo$" target="_blank" >doing</a> active-shooter drills in elementary school. We spent a chunk of our high school years staring at Zoom screens during the pandemic. We watched wildfires turn the sky orange while adults debated whether climate change was real. We grew up under a social media ecosystem that was <a class="Link" href="https://urldefense.com/v3/__https://www.hhs.gov/surgeongeneral/reports-and-publications/youth-mental-health/social-media/index.html__;!!KZXpRQ!QyuBQtA6hjtGb1hZbMhjAUvGF565co__MpN3Mt4v8NRU88igHEJqpR6NivE2xraGrOvPXGla5IaDXl1at24n8OA$" target="_blank" >running</a> experiments on our mental health. Now we're entering a job market that expects us to be grateful for the chance to compete over a shrinking slice of what’s left.</p><p>The pattern goes like this: A powerful industry emerges, moves fast, promises transformation and asks the public to trust that self-regulation will be enough. For a while, maybe it is. Then the harms start showing up — in our lungs, our app feeds and our schools. By the time anyone in Washington, D.C., actually does something about it, the kids who grew up breathing that air or scrolling those feeds are already carrying the consequences with them. We are living this with fossil fuel, social media and now with AI.</p><p>Fortunately, there is a common sense bill in Illinois that can proactively protect us before the harms snowball. The Artificial Intelligence Public Safety and Child Protection Transparency <a class="Link" href="https://ilga.gov/Legislation/BillStatus/FullText?GAID=18&DocNum=4705&DocTypeID=HB&LegId=165724&SessionID=114" target="_blank" >Act</a> isn't trying to kill AI or go after the companies building it. The point is simple: Get basic transparency and accountability in place now, while we still can.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-970000" name="module-970000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Commentary bug</div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h2>Commentary</h2></div> </div><p>California understood this need when it <a class="Link" href="https://urldefense.com/v3/__https://www.gov.ca.gov/2025/09/29/governor-newsom-signs-sb-53-advancing-californias-world-leading-artificial-intelligence-industry/__;!!KZXpRQ!QyuBQtA6hjtGb1hZbMhjAUvGF565co__MpN3Mt4v8NRU88igHEJqpR6NivE2xraGrOvPXGla5IaDXl1akMDoMhQ$" target="_blank" >enacted</a> SB 53 in September 2025, becoming the first state to require standardized safety disclosures from the developers of the most advanced AI systems. New York <a class="Link" href="https://urldefense.com/v3/__https://www.governor.ny.gov/news/governor-hochul-signs-nation-leading-legislation-require-ai-frameworks-ai-frontier-models__;!!KZXpRQ!QyuBQtA6hjtGb1hZbMhjAUvGF565co__MpN3Mt4v8NRU88igHEJqpR6NivE2xraGrOvPXGla5IaDXl1aM3yEf-s$" target="_blank" >followed</a> with the RAISE Act, creating its own reporting and governance framework for frontier models. Illinois now has the opportunity to join that growing coalition of states that decided the public deserves to know what the most powerful AI companies know and are doing about the risks of their own technology.</p><p>The core idea behind the Illinois bill is one that we already accept in virtually every other industry where the stakes are high. Pharmaceutical companies don't get to decide for themselves whether their drugs are safe. They’re <a class="Link" href="https://urldefense.com/v3/__https://www.fda.gov/safety/medwatch-fda-safety-information-and-adverse-event-reporting-program__;!!KZXpRQ!QyuBQtA6hjtGb1hZbMhjAUvGF565co__MpN3Mt4v8NRU88igHEJqpR6NivE2xraGrOvPXGla5IaDXl1aoGVVM_k$" target="_blank" >mandated</a> to publish clinical trial data, submit to independent audits and even report adverse events to an entire federal agency dedicated to exactly that. Airline companies don't get to investigate crashes in private — they, too, answer to an agency <a class="Link" href="https://urldefense.com/v3/__https://www.ntsb.gov/about/Pages/default.aspx__;!!KZXpRQ!QyuBQtA6hjtGb1hZbMhjAUvGF565co__MpN3Mt4v8NRU88igHEJqpR6NivE2xraGrOvPXGla5IaDXl1aQ_GPNq0$" target="_blank" >responsible</a> for that. It’s a clear precedent of oversight: Industries with the capacity to cause large-scale harm need external accountability structures.</p><p>If we demand this level of accountability from industries that have had decades to prove themselves, it's hard to see why we wouldn't expect at least the same from a technology that has been in the public's hands for less than three years — with the greatest potential to reshape how we live.</p><p>None of what the proposed legislation calls for is particularly radical. It simply <a class="Link" href="https://urldefense.com/v3/__https://www.ilga.gov/Legislation/BillStatus?DocNum=4705&GESSION=104&GA=104&DocTypeID=HB&LegID=&SessionID=114__;!!KZXpRQ!QyuBQtA6hjtGb1hZbMhjAUvGF565co__MpN3Mt4v8NRU88igHEJqpR6NivE2xraGrOvPXGla5IaDXl1ajxv8hdc$" target="_blank" ></a> says that the largest developers operating at the frontier of AI technology should document how their companies are thinking about catastrophic risks, publish a safety plan, report serious incidents to the attorney general and let independent auditors check their work. A company running a chatbot that a million minors can access needs a documented strategy for protecting them.</p><p>And if none of this is enough to make the case for acting now, then by default, we would be opting to repeat what has not worked for my generation — waiting while harms proliferate. Wait for another incident that <a class="Link" href="https://www.nytimes.com/2024/10/23/technology/characterai-lawsuit-teen-suicide.html?unlocked_article_code=1.bVA.Wzk6.5JtDu-ss3gmc&smid=url-share" target="_blank" >makes the front page</a> of the New York Times, wait for another tech CEO to <a class="Link" href="https://urldefense.com/v3/__https://www.npr.org/sections/shots-health-news/2025/09/19/nx-s1-5545749/ai-chatbots-safety-openai-meta-characterai-teens-suicide__;!!KZXpRQ!QyuBQtA6hjtGb1hZbMhjAUvGF565co__MpN3Mt4v8NRU88igHEJqpR6NivE2xraGrOvPXGla5IaDXl1anvwDNLU$" target="_blank" >testify</a> they’ll do better at congressional hearings, wait for the legislation that <a class="Link" href="https://urldefense.com/v3/__https://cbsaustin.com/news/nation-world/congress-mounts-another-push-for-child-online-safety-bills-kosa-social-media-artificial-intelligence-chatbots-big-tech-regulation__;!!KZXpRQ!QyuBQtA6hjtGb1hZbMhjAUvGF565co__MpN3Mt4v8NRU88igHEJqpR6NivE2xraGrOvPXGla5IaDXl1aEOr3XhI$" target="_blank" >arrives</a> years too late and applies to a landscape that has already moved on. We have lived through enough of those cycles to know that waiting is a choice, and a bad one.</p><p>More than anyone else, my generation will experience the ramifications of whatever AI becomes. We are the ones who will raise children in a world where these systems are likely integrated into every institution.</p><p> It's reasonable to ask our representatives to pass a bill that requires the most powerful AI companies to be honest about what they know, protect the kids using their products and let someone check their safety work. For a generation that grew up as the test subjects of the "move fast and figure it out later" approach, it might be one of the most reasonable things our legislators can do.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://urldefense.com/v3/__https://www.linkedin.com/in/kashyap-rajesh/__;!!KZXpRQ!TMbMY1yXajCnX7VvT5SQpQpMMbxeb44XlWj-jzHzbchyNAUjfx1ObBOrOT1U-OIW8d7-FBdzB_ds9V7W-d5eGoEN$" target="_blank" ><i>Kashyap Rajesh</i></a><i>, 19, grew up in Buffalo Grove and attends Cornell University. He is a member of </i><a class="Link" href="https://urldefense.com/v3/__https://designitforus.org/__;!!KZXpRQ!TMbMY1yXajCnX7VvT5SQpQpMMbxeb44XlWj-jzHzbchyNAUjfx1ObBOrOT1U-OIW8d7-FBdzB_ds9V7W-RimB8JN$" target="_blank" ><i>Design It For Us</i></a><i>, a youth-led advocacy organization working to counter Big Tech's influence in policy.</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-f20000" name="module-f20000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody">Send letters to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. More about <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/submitting-opinion-and-letters" >how to submit here</a>.</div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: Kashyap Rajesh

entries.title: Bears GM Ryan Poles has tough act to follow in this year's draft
entries.summary: <p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a> general manager Ryan Poles gave himself a tough act to follow.</p><p></p><p>Last year, in conjunction with new head coach Ben Johnson, Poles drafted tight end Colston Loveland, who led the Bears in both catches and receiving yards, in Round 1; Luther Burden, who ranked third among qualified NFL receivers in yards after catch per reception, in Round 2; and Kyle Monangai, who finished 24<sup>th</sup> in the league in rushing yards, all the way down in Round 7.</p><p></p><p>Second-round pick Ozzy Trapilo finished 31<sup>st</sup> of 84 qualifiers in Pro Football Focus’ offensive tackle grades, while sixth-rounder Luke Newman would have finished 30<sup>th</sup> of 79 guards had he played enough snaps.</p><p></p><p>Among 2025 classes, only the Browns compare. The Browns finished 5-12 last season, though. The Bears went 11-6 and won their first playoff game in 15 years.</p><p></p><p>Last year’s class might not even prove to be Poles’ best. If quarterback Caleb Williams becomes the franchise quarterback the Bears increasingly believe he can be — and if receiver Rome Odunze can stay healthy — then Poles’ 2024 first round will go down as nothing less than franchise-shifting.</p><p></p><p>Good luck following that in this year’s draft, which starts Thursday in Pittsburgh — especially when Poles isn't scheduled to draft until No. 25 overall. The Bears do hold four out of the first 89 picks, a product of trading receiver DJ Moore to the Bills for their second-round pick.</p><p></p><p>Poles’ fifth draft as Bears GM will be the most challenging of his career. For the first time since his burn-it-all-down first season, he doesn’t have a top-10 selection to use. For the first time ever, he has a playoff team to improve.</p><p></p><p>The Bears figure to lean just as heavily into defense this year as they did offense in their last draft. They’ll likely prioritize defensive end and defensive tackle, though they have starting jobs open at safety, linebacker and cornerback.</p><p></p><p>“You want to continue to have young talent come in and help us out on defense,” Poles told the Sun-Times at the NFL’s annual meeting last month. “Speaking out of both sides of my mouth — the hard part about the draft is drafting best available.”</p><p></p><p>Poles said that if all things are equal the Bears will “usually sequence [the draft] … to our needs.” The key, he said, is ensuring all things are equal without putting his finger on the scale.</p><p></p><p>Poles has a reputation for trading back — he’s only moved up once on draft day, and that was for second-round cornerback Tyrique Stevenson. In Round 1, though, he might be best-suited staying exactly where he is. Most draftniks believe there will be worthy defensive ends available at No. 25, from Missouri’s Zion Young to Auburn’s Keldric Faulk to Miami’s Akheem Mesidor.</p><p></p><p>“There’s going to be edge rushers there that make sense at 25,” ESPN draft guru Mel Kiper said Thursday. “It does line up, if they want the edge guy, at the right spot.”</p><p></p><p>Poles has done better than his predecessor did in Round 1.</p><p></p><p>From 2015-21, former Bears GM Ryan Pace made just five first-round picks. He got one right: linebacker Roquan Smith, the eighth pick in 2018, whom Poles later traded. None of Pace’s first-rounders — quarterbacks Justin Fields and Mitch Trubisky, edge rusher Leonard Floyd and one of the league’s greatest busts, receiver Kevin White — made it to a second contract with the Bears. Poles’ first-round picks — Williams, Odunze, Loveland and right tackle Darnell Wright — have already helped the Bears more than all of Pace’s first picks but Smith.</p><p></p><p>Nailing the 25<sup>th</sup> pick is a separate challenge than drafting in the top 10, though, which is why Poles has studied the Steelers and Ravens. Since 2000, the two teams have found defensive stars with the last 12 picks in Round 1 — Pro Football Hall of Fame safety Ed Reed and two future Hall of Famers, edge rusher TJ Watt and defensive tackle Cam Heyward.</p><p></p><p>When Bears fans linked arms in outage over the team <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/04/03/nfl-rejects-bears-appeal-to-receive-compensatory-draft-picks-for-falcons-hiring-ian-cunningham" target="_blank" >not receiving two third-round compensatory picks</a> for losing assistant GM Ian Cunningham to the Falcons, it was hard to fathom exactly what they were upset about losing. Poles’ third-round picks over four years have been abysmal — receiver-turned-running-back Velus Jones, who had 12 catches and four fumbles in two-and-a-half seasons; defensive tackle Zacch Pickens, who hit the quarterback exactly four times in two years; and injured Yale tackle Kiran Amegadjie, who started one game — a “Monday Night Football” massacre — in 2024. The three played zero snaps for the Bears last year.</p><p></p><p>Poles was wise, then, to trade his 2025 third-round selection last year in a six-pick swap. He might be tempted to do something similar this year. If he holds onto No. 89 this year, history shows there should be future Pro Bowl players available. In the last 15 years, receiver Nico Collins, defensive tackle Javon Hargrave and former Bears defensive tackle Akiem Hicks all were chosen in that spot.</p><p></p><p>If Poles can land an edge rusher in Round 1, he can use his three Day 2 picks on needs ranging from defensive tackle to safety, cornerback to center — or maybe even a developmental left tackle. Kiper believes the team could snag a useful receiver on Day 3, too.</p><p></p><p>“I think this draft does line up — with the way their needs are and the way the board sets up — to allow them to have a pretty productive draft,” he said.<br></p>
entries.author: Patrick Finley

entries.title: Burdened by costs of an ICE facility in town, Broadview reckons with a spotlight it never wanted
entries.summary: <p>In a lot of ways, what happened last fall to the small village of Broadview during President Donald Trump’s immigration “blitz” felt like a storm.</p><p>Broadview’s leaders and residents had no say in the scope or magnitude of the federal push that shuffled thousands of immigrants through the U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement facility that’s long operated in their quiet town. They couldn’t control what was coming or predict the impact.</p><p>The 7,900 people living in the unassuming western suburb are still digging out.</p><p>Just as the village had gotten its financial house in order, it was hit with nearly $400,000 in unexpected costs as a result of last fall’s campaign — around 10% of the small municipality's discretionary budget. That’s in sharp contrast to local governments elsewhere in the country that have contracted with the federal government to have their costs covered — and more — for detaining immigrants in local jails.</p><p>Broadview’s mayor recently made an ambitious request for federal reimbursement — that the village’s budget is banking on — and announced grand plans to have the ICE facility shuttered to become a museum to help change Broadview’s image. But her requests are a long shot.</p><p>Regionally, Broadview is now synonymous with the federal immigration processing facility at 1930 Beach Street that last fall became a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2025/10/01/broadview-immigration-processing-center-detention-ice-dhs" target="_blank" ><u>de facto immigration detention site</u></a> and drew the area’s most contentious<b> </b>protests.</p><p>Reluctant to draw more attention to themselves or their village, many residents say they don’t want to publicly discuss the saga.</p><p>Broadview Mayor Katrina Thompson also canceled a scheduled interview with the Chicago Sun-Times and WBEZ after learning the village finance director answered a reporter’s budget questions without her knowledge. She sent word through spokesman David Ormsby who says, “There is currently no intent to reschedule.”</p><p>Still, after the Illinois legislature recently tried to restrict immigration enforcement facilities in residential areas, Thompson took to social media and wrote, “No community should have to experience what the Village of Broadview has endured.</p><p>“How do we ensure that no community is left to carry a burden it neither chose nor has the power to control?”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-3e0000" name="image-3e0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0d7b4cb/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4620x2593+0+245/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb7%2Fb6%2F8fb8d7f2485aaa690a4e6e74dd22%2Fice-092025-32.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2aede8a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4620x2593+0+245/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb7%2Fb6%2F8fb8d7f2485aaa690a4e6e74dd22%2Fice-092025-32.jpg class="Figure-credit"><p>Ashlee Rezin/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><b>Before the “blitz,” Broadview flew under the radar </b></h3><p>The village is the picture of calm, with tree-lined residential streets and unpretentious shopping corridors. Until last fall, it generated few headlines — for anything, really.</p><p>At 1.78 square miles, it’s smaller than most Chicago wards. What might characterize this suburb just south of I-290 at 25th Avenue, is just how modest it is.</p><p>Many Broadview residents say they moved there for that peace, some for retirement, others for the lower costs.</p><p>The most expensive home listed online for sale in the village where it’s hard to find houses bigger than three bedrooms is $310,000.</p><div class="Enhancement" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Broadview is a quiet community of modest-sized homes, including this row of houses on 22nd Avenue. It’s hard to find a home there with more than three bedrooms.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The U.S. Census pegged the village’s median income at $65,000 in 2024, putting it in the lowest 25% of Cook County municipalities, below Chicago’s $77,000. Yet its share of poor people is just 9%.</p><p>That’s influenced by a large number of seniors in Broadview — about one in every five residents — whose income is typically fixed.</p><p>Broadview’s total population peaked in 1970, back when Black residents accounted for less than 6% of the 9,600 mostly white people living there.</p><p>But in similar trends around Chicago, demographics began to flip and the Black population quickly rose to 74% by 2000. Black residents still comprise a majority with Latinos making up about a quarter, and white residents accounting for 11%.</p><p>Unlike Chicago’s south suburbs, whose property tax rates ballooned after their industrial bases slipped away, Broadview stabilized property tax rates at an average 10.5% that matches Cook County’s median, according to 2024 data.</p><p>That’s because as this and many other suburbs were booming following World War II, Broadview made sure its land would remain evenly diversified. It’s still roughly a third residential, a third industrial and a third commercial — including the Broadview Village Square shopping center and the classic Sawa's Old Warsaw Restaurant.</p><p>Then the federal “blitz” upended the suburb’s under-the-radar status.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-5f0000" name="image-5f0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/13a73b5/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7533x4228+0+398/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fef%2Fb3%2Ff4c7ba3b41a8bcbbfcbb3877b295%2Fice-092025-24-3.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b9105d7/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7533x4228+0+398/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fef%2Fb3%2Ff4c7ba3b41a8bcbbfcbb3877b295%2Fice-092025-24-3.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c8e2881/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7533x4228+0+398/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fef%2Fb3%2Ff4c7ba3b41a8bcbbfcbb3877b295%2Fice-092025-24-3.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="A federal immigration enforcement agent sprays Rev.
