David Black, of the First Presbyterian Church of Chicago, as he and other protesters demonstrate outside the ICE facility in Broadview on Sept. 19, 2025. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Ashlee Rezin/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The ICE building operating for decades at 1930 Beach Street became the center of the action and resulting protests. From its roof, masked federal agents lobbed tear gas, pepper spray and rubber projectiles. They barricaded Beach Street with a 8-foot-high fence.</p><p>The mayor declared a state of emergency.</p><p>Neighbors suffered the effects of the chemicals in and around their homes.</p><p>During the last week of September, a resident some 300 yards<b> </b>from the ICE building told lawyers the pepper spray aggravated his asthma at his house. Street blockages also prevented Reggie Thompson from getting groceries delivered or his home’s electricity serviced after it went out, court records show.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-380000" name="image-380000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5fe6585/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5575x3129+0+295/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa6%2F96%2F3c73d0da4567a5135ae4922196bb%2Fbroadview-101225-23.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0a0a60b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5575x3129+0+295/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa6%2F96%2F3c73d0da4567a5135ae4922196bb%2Fbroadview-101225-23.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/22e5d86/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5575x3129+0+295/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa6%2F96%2F3c73d0da4567a5135ae4922196bb%2Fbroadview-101225-23.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Illinois State Police troopers push protesters refusing to disperse on 25th Avenue near the ICE facility shortly before the city of Broadview’s 6 p.m. protest curfew on Oct. 11, 2025. They declined to comment. Nearby, Kevin Halm of the Northcross Roofing construction company says protesters took up his parking spaces and blocked the lot’s entrance.</p><p>And as the weeks went by, fewer diners were walking into the Broadview Family Restaurant about four blocks from the ICE facility, says Ricardo Padilla, one of the employees.</p><p>“People have been really scared away, and so basically it slowed down the business,” Padilla says. “I think as a whole, it just put the village on a podium, and it’s like every single move just pretty much … it’s mainly just negative.”</p><div class="Enhancement" He says the activity at the facility hurt the restaurant and the village.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><b>Broadview had its finances together — until the “blitz” </b></h3><p>One year ago, Broadview officials spent all of 15 minutes discussing the village’s 2025-26 budget at a public hearing. Armed with a surplus and millions more in cash reserves, the village’s finance director called the spending plan “one of the easiest ones I’ve done in a while.”</p><p>Fast forward, and the cheery outlook is gone.</p><p>What started as a $334,000 surplus on core operations turned into a $94,000 deficit for the fiscal year ending April 30, budget documents show.</p><p>That’s because midway through the fiscal year, the village had to spend $361,536 responding to activity around the ICE facility through mid-January, Broadview officials say.</p><p>Records obtained by the Sun-Times through a Freedom of Information Act request show those costs included $71,185 police overtime, $12,100 for fire department overtime and $3,000 for additional personal protective equipment. Ambulance transfers from the ICE facility’s address cost nearly $250,000.</p><p>Another $41,000 went toward legal expenses, mostly to sue the feds over the fence they erected near the facility — without permission — that blocked off Beach Street.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>The most expensive expense connected to the influx of immigrants into the Broadview ICE facility last fall was $250,000 for ambulance transfers from the center, records show. The village also spent $41,000 on legal expenses to sue the federal government over the fence they built without permission. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times file</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>In a new $22 million operating budget proposal unveiled this week for the new fiscal year starting May 1, Broadview officials are leaving room for costs related to the ICE facility even though the feds won’t say if they’re sending another <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2026/03/10/chicago-immigration-deport-trump-noem-bovino-midway-blitz" >wave of agents back to Chicago</a>.</p><p>Those include more for overtime and for the clerk’s office to handle an influx of FOIA requests — which landed in Broadview as the federal government limited its release of information. Separately, contracts require pay raises for police officers, firefighters and other village employees. Health care costs are up 17%.</p><p>The surplus projected for next year is just $45,000, according to the proposed budget.</p><p>The village expects to have to dip into its reserves — even after assuming hundreds of thousands in reimbursement from the federal or state governments for ICE-related municipal expenses.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" fiscal year that begins on May 1.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>"It's costing us a ton,” finance director Thomas Hood told trustees this week. “So it's really led to a lot of expense for the village that we didn't count on. But we're hopeful that we're going to get reimbursed for some of that."</p><p>Justin Marlowe, Director of the Center for Municipal Finance at the University of Chicago, says, “that's a lot of money riding on something that's less than certain.”</p><p>The vast majority of Broadview’s budget — around 80% — is eaten up by police and fire department salaries and predetermined pension payments. That leaves around $4 million for discretionary spending on village programs. Last year, costs resulting from ICE facility activity ate up about 10% of this.</p><p>“If we weren't already in a great financial position, we'd be underwater now,” Hood told the Sun-Times. He calls the hundreds of thousands of dollars in unexpected spending a “huge deal.”</p><p>“It throws a wrench into your plans,” he says. “Plans that had been in place and going for maybe five years or even longer."</p><h3>Most other towns with ICE facilities, like in Indiana, don’t incur costs </h3><p>Nationally, Broadview stands out as one of only a few immigration centers that the federal government owns and operates. Most others are local jails<b> </b>that lease space to the federal government for a daily flat fee for each person detained there for alleged immigration violations.</p><p>One of the closest is in Clay County, Ind., a three-and-a-half-hour drive, where local taxpayers have at the very least been shielded from increases in related costs resulting from detaining more immigrants, such as a surge in ambulance runs to nearby hospitals.</p><p>That’s because Clay County collects $85 per day per immigrant, according to its contract with the U.S. Marshals Service. The deal has prompted lawsuits from immigrant-rights advocates accusing Clay County of profiting handsomely from increased detentions.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-e50000" name="image-e50000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d0cedc9/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5173x2903+0+273/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F3c%2Fc9%2F6fe2a9b2447f9ade4a926c42ee6c%2Fclay-county-jail-ext-03-h.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b93c327/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5173x2903+0+273/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F3c%2Fc9%2F6fe2a9b2447f9ade4a926c42ee6c%2Fclay-county-jail-ext-03-h.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b2db943/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5173x2903+0+273/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F3c%2Fc9%2F6fe2a9b2447f9ade4a926c42ee6c%2Fclay-county-jail-ext-03-h.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Clay County Jail Ext 03 - H.jpg" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b2db943/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5173x2903+0+273/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F3c%2Fc9%2F6fe2a9b2447f9ade4a926c42ee6c%2Fclay-county-jail-ext-03-h.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3dba171/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5173x2903+0+273/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F3c%2Fc9%2F6fe2a9b2447f9ade4a926c42ee6c%2Fclay-county-jail-ext-03-h.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b2db943/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5173x2903+0+273/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F3c%2Fc9%2F6fe2a9b2447f9ade4a926c42ee6c%2Fclay-county-jail-ext-03-h.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>The Clay County Jail in Brazil, Ind., near Terre Haute, receives $85 a day from the federal government for each immigrant detained.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Records show Clay County has had a detention-for-hire agreement in place since 2006, at $45 per federal inmate then. Terms expanded in 2013 to allow ICE to use the jail for immigrants pending deportation, and the daily rate later rose to $55.</p><p>Meanwhile in 2021, Illinois’ legislature barred such agreements between law enforcement and federal immigration officials, ending contracts between local governments including Kankakee County.</p><p>Clay County’s jail was the only long-term immigrant detention center in Indiana by 2022 when<b> </b>county officials voted to build an addition, expanding its capacity from 176 beds to more than 450.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div County officials voted in 2022 to more than double its capacity to 450 beds.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Two of Clay County’s self-described “very conservative” commissioners viewed the proposed $25 million expansion as a smart investment for county taxpayers as ICE sought more detention space, they explained on local radio in December 2021.</p><p>Clay County had already reaped benefits of adding ICE inmates, a way to “make more money for the county” whenever its population of local inmates dropped, commissioner Marty Heffner said.</p><p>“The $55 a day, we are definitely making a profit at that,” Heffner continued. The rate since has risen to $85 a day.</p><p>Fellow commissioner Paul Sinders said the goal is to keep local taxes down “and not to increase taxes.”</p><p>“Because if we receive more ICE detainees, we speculate that we could receive close to $7 million per year for ICE detainees,” Sinders said. “And this will pay for the expenses of the jail, salaries, utilities, heating, cooling, and so on and and without that money then being freed up, the county may have to raise taxes.”</p><p>Heffner, Sinders and the county sheriff didn’t respond to interview requests.</p><p>Some outside help was made available to Broadview. Last October, Illinois State Police and the Cook County Sheriff and others sent officers to help Broadview police respond to protests that grew chaotic when federal agents fired gas and projectiles. State police are still calculating their share of costs for 34,000 staff hours; the sheriff’s office has spent about $3.8 million, agency spokespeople said.</p><p></p><h3><b>Mayor looks to set a new 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/57e8338/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5998x3366+0+317/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F77%2Fa5%2Fb202ebdf49408e2a74fab4414d34%2Flegisadvance-101425-01.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b7dd03d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5998x3366+0+317/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F77%2Fa5%2Fb202ebdf49408e2a74fab4414d34%2Flegisadvance-101425-01.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Broadview Mayor Katrina Thompson, who has tried to retain control over her village as its been thrust onto a national stage, leaves a press conference outside the ICE processing center on Oct. 13, 2025.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>In her latest attempt to move Broadview out of an unwanted spotlight, Thompson has asked members of the Illinois congressional delegation for help securing reimbursement plus the closure of the ICE facility and its transformation into a museum.</p><p>Is that possible?</p><p>“If the federal government really wants to do that, they can find a way to do it, and they can find a way to do it relatively quickly," the University of Chicago’s Marlowe says.</p><p>Political will is another matter.</p><p>The Trump administration has sought a dramatic expansion of ICE facilities nationwide, including efforts to purchase commercial warehouses to open new detention centers. So closing facilities doesn’t seem in the cards. The two senators and six representatives whom Thompson enlisted for help are all Democrats — unlikely candidates to sway the administration.</p><p>Ormsby, Thompson’s spokesman, says the mayor has not yet received any responses<b>.</b> The only offices to respond to requests for comment were Rep. Sean Casten’s, which said he never received a letter from the mayor but would be open to helping, and Sen. Tammy Duckworth’s, which said it received the mayor’s letter and is in touch with Broadview officials.</p><p>For those living in Broadview, the costs went beyond the financial.</p><p>Last fall, a resident and business owner railed to her village’s defense in a Facebook post boosted by the mayor, begging people to stop saying, “Broadview ICE Detention Center.”</p><p>“Friends, I need your help to stop the slander against Broadview,” she wrote in September. “We are NOT like the towns that have prisons and give jobs to the community. We are a small, proud, majority Black working and middle-class community with a high quality of life.”</p><p>“Please help me stop people from disparaging my Village.”</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/24/broadview-mayor-demands-closure-of-ice-facility-reimbursement-of-village-costs-business-losses-police-fire-congress-trump-detention" >Broadview mayor demands closure of ICE facility, reimbursement of village, business losses</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2025/10/01/broadview-immigration-processing-center-detention-ice-dhs" >ICE’s Broadview facility has become a de facto detention center, minus the rules and oversight</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/immigration/2025/10/04/broadview-mayor-katrina-thompson-ice-detention-center-arrests-kristi-noem-gregory-bovino" >Amid national glare, Broadview mayor keeps focus on her 8,000 residents: ‘I can’t be afraid’</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Nader Issa

entries.title: Life on the road inspired Chicago band Friko’s sophomore album ‘Something Worth Waiting For’
entries.summary: <p>Friko’s sophomore album could be described as the musical equivalent of “Planes, Trains and Automobiles.” Except that the band’s unending touring journey from Chicago and back again the past couple of years entailed trains, vans, bikes and hot air balloons.</p><p>“Life and traveling were the most overarching influences of this album,” explained singer and guitarist Niko Kapetan of “Something Worth Waiting For,” which the band will promote with an in-store appearance at Shuga Records on April 19 and a record release show at Metro on April 25.</p><p>Out April 24 on ATO Records, the transient-natured album is carried by songs like the indie mad dash of “Choo Choo,” the pensive charmer “Dear Bicycle” and the soft hum of “Hot Air Balloon,” the latter of which was inspired by the band waking up one morning in an Albuquerque Cracker Barrel parking lot to see the colorful orbs of the city’s International Balloon Fiesta filling the sky.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-ce0000" name="image-ce0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f792693/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3788x2126+0+831/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F76%2Fd4%2Fd4ef9059423ea952dcb4f23b7a72%2Ffriko-something-worth-waiting-for-album-cover-artwork-copy.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/eb7bf88/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3788x2126+0+831/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F76%2Fd4%2Fd4ef9059423ea952dcb4f23b7a72%2Ffriko-something-worth-waiting-for-album-cover-artwork-copy.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/67fa287/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3788x2126+0+831/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F76%2Fd4%2Fd4ef9059423ea952dcb4f23b7a72%2Ffriko-something-worth-waiting-for-album-cover-artwork-copy.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/eb7bf88/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3788x2126+0+831/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F76%2Fd4%2Fd4ef9059423ea952dcb4f23b7a72%2Ffriko-something-worth-waiting-for-album-cover-artwork-copy.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>“Something Worth Waiting For,” out on April 24 on ATO Records, is Friko’s second album.</p></figcaption></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“We were writing whenever we were home, which wasn’t too much … and it really came from going around the world and the people we’d meet along the way,” Kapetan said. And there have been plenty of opportunities the past few years.</p><p>After incubating in Chicago’s DIY rock community, in 2023, Friko was swept up in a wave of national attention on the scene and a signing frenzy of local bands (they’re on the Dave Matthews-founded ATO Records, the same label as fellow Chicagoans Brigitte Calls Me Baby and Neal Francis).</p><p>With the release of the genre-bending debut album, “Where We’ve Been, Where We Go From Here” in 2024 (featuring the hit “Get Numb To It!”), Friko instantly lit up the blogosphere as declarations of “the best of the year” and “a band to watch” poured in.<b> </b>Soon after, the members were plucked from their apartments in Logan Square, Ukrainian Village and Humboldt Park and thrust onto an international stage with barely any time in between to come home and unpack.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-5a0000" name="module-5a0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>Catch up with Friko</h3><br>Here’s where you can meet the band this week and next.<br><br><b>In-store signing</b><br><b>When:</b> 3 p.m. April 19<br><b>Where:</b> Shuga Records, 1272 N Milwaukee Ave.<br><b>Tickets:</b> Free<br><b>Info:</b> <a class="Link" href="https://www.shugarecords.com/" target="_blank" >shugarecords.com</a><br><br><b>Record release show</b><br><b>When:</b> 8 p.m. April 25<br><b>Where:</b> Metro, 3730 N. Clark<br><b>Tickets:</b> $29 (in advance)<br><b>Info:</b> <a class="Link" href="https://metrochicago.com/" target="_blank" >metrochicago.com</a><br></div> </div><p>In addition to going on tour with indie godfathers The Flaming Lips and Modest Mouse, there have been appearances at the Newport Folk Festival, Lollapalooza and Pitchfork Music Festival editions in London and Paris as well as an unexpected boon in Japan not unlike fellow Illinois band Cheap Trick did in the 1970s.</p><p><b></b> “I don’t know if we’re at that level,” Kapetan joked of the comparison to Rockford’s finest. “We got lucky with the first record; it blew up on Japanese Twitter, and we got the opportunity to play Fuji Rock Festival [in July], which opened this opportunity with China and Taiwan, too. We're very grateful,” he added regarding a short tour in Asia in late 2024. <b></b></p><p>Having these big career moments was certainly nothing Kapetan and drummer Bailey Minzenberger expected when the two dreamed up the band after graduating Evanston Township High School in 2018.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/music/2024/02/26/chicago-band-friko-accelerates-its-ascent-with-dazzling-debut-album" >Chicago band Friko accelerates its ascent with dazzling debut album</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/reset-with-sasha-ann-simons/2023/05/05/fresh-off-a-monster-tour-the-trio-talks-songwriting-and-chicagos-music-scene" target="_blank" >Chicago rising stars Friko perform live at WBEZ</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/music/2024/06/14/local-chicago-music-scene-rocking-again-getting-national-industry-attention" >Chicago music scene is rocking again, and it’s getting national attention</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>Kapetan said the band owes “everything to the Chicago scene and coming up in it. And it’s so cool now that bands like Ratboys and Dehd and Whitney, we’re able to befriend them, because we loved them in high school.”</p><p>“There’s just so much good music happening everywhere, all the time, that it’s hard not to be inspired,” Minzenberger added. “If you need a little pick-me-up, you can just go see a show on any given night, and you’re probably going to see your friends at that show.”</p><p>It’s how they came upon new bassist David Fuller, who was a mutual friend’s recommendation after original bassist Luke Stamos left, and guitarist Korgan Robb, who had displayed his chops in other Chicago bands like The Courts. Though the two joined the ensemble on recent tours, “Something Worth Waiting For” is the first time the newly inked quartet recorded together, which makes the band “more powerful, in a positive way,” said Kapetan, and also helped them avoid any notion of a sophomore slump.</p><p><b> </b>Further signaling this new era, Friko recently wiped clean its Instagram account. “It wasn't really like a big brain board meeting thing necessarily,” said Fuller, who runs the band’s social media accounts. “We’re not running away from the past. And we’ve archived everything.” <b></b></p><p>“But there’s a much clearer image with this record and how we present it,” Kapetan said. Like the album’s song material, it all hinges on movement, whether it’s the band’s sonic evolution from chamber folk and indie pop to more noisey fuzz, symphonic leaps and retro-tinged ballads, or the literal movement like filming the “Choo Choo” video on the CTA and hunkering down in a nondescript Midwest field for cinematic counterpart to “Seven Degrees.”</p><p>The name of the song refers to Kapetan’s numeric mix-up over the “six degrees of separation” theory. As its profile has grown, the band has vastly expanded its network in recent years, especially a growing friendship/mentorship with The Flaming Lips’ Wayne Coyne.</p><p>“It was really inspiring being on the road with him. And it kind of gave us ideas of what a future could look like if we give our full heart over to this,” recalled Fuller.</p><p>“But the best thing he ever told us,” added Robb, “is that the gods of music are always watching.”</p>
entries.author: Selena Fragassi

entries.title: Power Rising Summit draws Kamala Harris and Juliana Stratton to harness the collective power of Black women
entries.summary: <p>A national gathering of Black women that centers collective power and resilience starts today in Chicago. The Power Rising Summit will be held from Friday to Sunday at the Hilton Chicago.</p><p>The summit’s founder, Bishop Leah Daughtry, said bringing the summit in its ninth year to Chicago is a nod to “the leadership here that exists in Chicago. Chicago has always been a linchpin for the African-American community nationwide.”</p><p>On Friday, the summit features a conversation with Chicago Mayor Brandon Johnson and a panel discussion with attorney and podcast host Angela Rye and political strategist Donna Brazile. Cook County Clerk Monica Gordon, Illinois Lt. Gov. Juliana Stratton, and U.S. Rep. Yvette Clark (D-NY) are among the women on Saturday’s agenda. And the summit will culminate with a Sunday Soul Brunch featuring former Vice President Kamala D. Harris.</p><p>Other local speakers include U.S. Reps. Lauren Underwood (D-Ill.) and Robin Kelly (D-Ill.); Jennifer Ballard Croft, general counsel of Resource Innovations, Inc.; Cherita Ellens, president and CEO of Women Employed; Keiana Barrett, CEO of the Business Leadership Council; Tiffany Hightower, CEO of the Illinois Legislative Black Caucus Foundation; Katrina Jackson; and Watchen Nyanue Hampton, CEO of I Choose the Ladder.</p><p>Power Rising is built on five pillars: business and economic empowerment, health and wellness, culture and community, political empowerment, and education and technology. Workshops during the summit will focus on these key issues that may speak to the diversity of interests and needs of attendees.</p><p>“We try to speak to the totality of our experience. So some women come and they’re into technology. There's something for them,” Daughtry said. “For some women, they’ve been displaced by the economic times. There’s a workshop for them to talk about how they pivot. Some of us are political animals. Some of us are into the resistance and how do we fight back?”</p><p>This year’s summit is co-covened by Daughtry, chairperson of the 2008 Democratic National Convention, and Minyon Moore, chairperson of the 2024 DNC, held in Chicago.</p><p>On Thursday, ahead of the 2026 midterm elections, Power Rising launched 1M Black Voters Rising, an <a class="Link" href="https://www.onemillionblackvotersrising.org/" target="_blank" ><u>initiative</u></a> to register one million new voters. It’s also an effort to galvanize and activate people, and encourage people to check their voter registration status.</p><p>Organizations supporting the initiative include the National Council of Negro Women, Higher Heights for America PAC, Black Womens Roundtable, all four Black Greek-letter sororities of the National Pan-Hellenic Council, and Win with Black Women, the organization that mobilized a call with 44,000 women and raised $1.5 million dollars within hours of Harris’s 2024 presidential run announcement.</p><p>Daughtry said 1M Black Voters Rising “will be a way that you will see Black women rowing in the same direction, working together to organize people ahead of the midterms, doing the civic and voter education that needs to happen.”</p><p>Historically, Black women voters have been a politically engaged group. Their voter turnout nationally has hovered near and beyond 60% since the passage of the Voting Rights Act of 1965, according to data from the U.S. Census Bureau. And since 2008, average turnout for Black women is higher than the average turnout for all voters.</p><p>The initiative hopes to target younger voters among members of some of the partnering organizations.</p><p>Younger generations often express disenchantment with elections, and Daughtry says Gen Z is no different.</p><p>“I think every generation has its share of disenchantment. I got X's and Z's disenchanted because our government, quite frankly, hasn't given us much to be enchanted about,” Daughtry said. “The systems haven't worked for us, it seems. And so, people have problems.”</p><p>Ten percent of voters in the 2022 midterm elections were 18 to 29 years old, according to the Pew Research Center. In the 2024 elections, 15% of voters were in that age group, but so were 30% of nonvoters — the largest share of nonvoters across all age groups.</p><p>Nevertheless, Daughtry said the U.S. is a country that runs on a political system.</p><p>“I can’t expect to make change if I’m not going to engage in the system that this country runs on,” she said. “If I buy a car, but I don’t like gas, I’m not going nowhere. The only way that car is running is if I put some gas in the thing. To me, it’s the same thing with elections.”</p><p>Daughtry says she understands why Black voters, in particular, may be reluctant to vote, but it's a feeling that should be overcome.</p><p>“What’s happened in this country over our existence has given us much to be skeptical about,” Daughtry said. “Each generation has to decide what that looks like for them and how they want to make change.”<br></p>
entries.author: Nicole Jeanine Johnson

entries.title: Inside Mag Mile's upscale magic theater The Hand & The Eye
entries.summary: <p>A high-end magic theater — a $50 million passion project from entrepreneur Glen Tullman — will open in Streeterville on Saturday. </p><p>The Hand & The Eye requires visitors to shell out at least $225 and don their finest threads for a three-hour experience at the 35,000-square-foot McCormick Mansion, 100 E. Ontario St. </p><p>There’s no main stage or audience at The Hand & The Eye, Tullman said. Instead, it’s a series of experiences that include entering secret passageways and viewing intimate performances by highly-skilled magicians. </p><p>“There are big theaters that you can go to and see magic performed,” he said. “That's not what this is about. You're a part of the magic. You're experiencing it yourself. It's happening to you in your hands and around you.”</p><p>Tullman doesn’t think another venue like his exists in the world, in terms of size and scope. He said the closest experience is possibly the Magic Castle, the Los Angeles magician's club where guests must be invited or accompanied by a member. </p><p>The McCormick Mansion, decorated with a 1920s flair, is completely bejeweled, with each room displaying different wallpapers and designs. Velvet and gold accents are as plentiful as the secret passageways. </p><p>There's five performance spaces, two dining rooms and seven bars, along with a strict cocktail attire dress code. </p><p>Tickets start at $225, which includes a $75 dining credit. Memberships will soon be available, with perks like access to more rooms inside the mansion.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Glen Tullman holds The Hand & The Eye playing cards.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Tullman, CEO of health tech platform Transcarent, has a long history in the health care industry and is a serial entrepreneur. He founded Livongo, a consumer digital health company that was acquired by Teledoc Health in 2020, and was one of the founders of River North-based venture capital fund 7Wire Ventures. </p><p>But another of his passions is magic. Tullman said he started practicing when he was 8 years old and considers himself an amateur magician.</p><p>“Whether I saw young people or old people, men or women, all ethnicities, everyone loved magic,” Tullman, 66, said. “And there was this sense of returning them back to this time when you had complete focus and just a sense of joy that was being created.”</p><p>He had been been looking for an “opportunity to create the best place to experience magic in the entire world,” and found the McCormick Mansion, once the long-time home of Lawry's The Prime Rib before it <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2020/9/20/21448518/lawrys-the-prime-rib-to-close-at-end-of-year-after-46-years-in-chicago" >closed in 2020</a>. Tullman worked with architect David Rockwell, who has designed Nobu restaurants and the Dolby Theatre, home of the Academy Awards, in Los Angeles. </p><p>Tullman said the new venture is a chance “to give back to Chicago and the world of magic.” </p><p>Bre Smith, The Hand & The Eye’s executive vice president, said about 20 to 30 magicians will perform each evening. Guest capacity will start at around 250 people, she said.</p><p>“This is really building that third home,” Smith said. “It's taking you away from work. It's taking you away from your home and coming to a place where you completely disconnect. There are very limited windows in the mansion. We don't allow photos … and you hardly see any illuminated screens.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f8aeebe/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6284x3527+0+361/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd3%2F5a%2F428d32744769b6f3253c3153000c%2Fmagic-260417-013.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c804b05/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6284x3527+0+361/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd3%2F5a%2F428d32744769b6f3253c3153000c%2Fmagic-260417-013.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>The Hand & The Eye Executive Vice President Bre Smith</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The venue has a magic collection, which includes items from famous magicians like Harry Houdini, as well as a retail store where guests can shop and learn their own tricks.</p><p>“Glen gave me the key and basically said, ‘Build the experience,’” Smith said.</p><p>Jim Steinmeyer, an illusionist and magic historian who has served as the president of Magic Castle, said The Hand & The Eye is "incredibly ambitious." Steinmeyer has served as a magic consultant to heavyweights like Siegfried & Roy and David Copperfield.</p><p>"Magic looks great when the audience is close to it,” Steinmeyer said. “You want to be close enough to appreciate [it], and you don't want to be looking from the back row."</p><p>Over the last decade, more magic clubs and theaters have been opening but each are very different, he said. </p><p>Steinmeyer said ultimately, the real test will be how the public responds to The Hand & The Eye and whether they want to keep coming back. </p><p>”It’s admirable that the hospitality aspect and the magic are both of a high quality,” he said. "They're trying to match the quality of the performance with the quality of the dinner, with the quality of the environment. That's ambitious and that shows a lot of respect for the public.”</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/columnists/2026/04/16/food-delivery-robots-coco-chicago-bus-shelter-mishaps-hand-and-the-eye-magic" >Technology always wins, but we can make our own magic until then</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Mariah Rush

entries.title: Should Chicago continue to have delivery robots on city streets? Here's what you told us
entries.summary: <p>We asked readers whether Chicago <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/business/2026/delivery-robots-chicago-food-coco-robotics" >should continue to have delivery robots on the city’s streets</a>. Here's what you said, lightly edited for clarity:</p><p>"Yes, because they're less likely to kill a bystander than an Uber Eats driver staring at their phone behind the wheel."<br><i>— Collin Pearsall</i></p><p></p><p>"Chicago should not have delivery robots. These food delivery jobs are typically the types of jobs that our youth would have as they were finishing high school or during the summer. Our kids, and adults for that matter, could benefit from these jobs. It doesn't seem very smart to allow bots to take over the workforce, no matter how small it starts. Additionally, the property damage will only increase, and soon, more people will be injured too."</p><p><i>— Dee Crawford</i></p><p>"Delivery robots are part of our future. I support them. There have been very few glitches so far, and as far as I know, no one has been injured. The technology will continue to improve. Within a year or two, I doubt anyone will think twice about them."</p><p><i>— David Shiner</i></p><p>"Get these nuisances off our sidewalks."</p><p><i>— Bill Dahl</i></p><p>"Delivery robots and autonomous vehicles are just a way for tech companies to drain money out of Chicago and lay off workers. There is no benefit to the local economy."</p><p><i>— Mike McMains</i></p><p></p><p>"I would take more delivery robots on our sidewalks over the current situation. ... Our streets are routinely choked with double-parked delivery drivers. A drive along Lincoln Avenue between Webster and Fullerton any evening of the week is chaos."</p><p><i>— Tom Stroud</i></p><p>"Sure, any new technology has safety issues. My grandparents said their parents tried to stop the switchover to cars from horses. My grandparents envisioned giant planes crashing into schools and football stadiums. My parents thought microwaves would cook our brains."</p><p><i>— Linda Gies</i></p><p>"Yes, they’re cute."<br><i>— Valerie Morrow</i> </p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/business/2026/delivery-robots-chicago-food-coco-robotics" >What to know about Chicago’s delivery robots and the debate over them</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Sun-Times staff

entries.title: Storms possible as 'summer-like' conditions give way to much cooler temperatures Saturday in Chicago area
entries.summary: <p>"Summer-like" conditions bringing temperatures nearing 80 and a threat for severe storms Friday will give way to much cooler temperatures as the weekend progresses. </p><p>Highs were expected to reach 81 degrees Friday with a threat for large hail and damaging winds between 40 and 50 mph approaching by the evening, according to the National Weather Service. </p><p>"This could be our last summer-like day here for a little while," said Kevin Donofrio, a meteorologist with the weather service.</p><p>By Saturday, the high is expected be 30 degrees cooler than Friday, and by Saturday night temperatures could hover just above the freezing point, according to the weather service. A slight chance for rain was possible and wind gusts were expected to reach 30 mph. </p><p>Donofrio noted that a cold front moving through the central part of the United States is responsible for the approaching storm system and swing in temperature. </p><p>"Temperatures in the northern plains are in the 20s and 30s and that's pushing in our direction," Donofrio said. </p><p>Conditions are expected to be calm Sunday and Monday and highs in the 70s were expected to return by Tuesday. <br></p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Teen killed after crashing vehicle in Rolling Meadows industrial park
entries.summary: <p>An 18-year-old man was killed when police say he crashed his vehicle in a Rolling Meadows industrial park late Wednesday.</p><p>Rolling Meadows police said the crash happened just after 9:45 p.m. on the 3600 block of Industrial Avenue.</p><p>The driver was identified by the Cook County medical examiner’s office as Emilio D. Amaro. No hometown was given.</p><p>Police said Amaro was driving west on Euclid Avenue approaching Rohlwing Road with his vehicle’s headlights off when he passed a marked patrol car headed north on Rohlwing at Euclid.</p><p>For more on this story, please visit <a class="Link" href="https://www.dailyherald.com/20260417/news/teen-killed-after-crashing-vehicle-in-rolling-meadows-industrial-park/" target="_blank" >DailyHerald.com</a>.</p>
entries.author: Daily Herald

entries.title: CPS will be in session on May 1, but agreement with CTU encourages students to attend afternoon rally
entries.summary: <p>Chicago Public Schools will be in session for a full day on May 1, but it will also be an official civic day of action during which hundreds of students will be able to take a “field trip” to a massive pro-labor, anti-President Donald Trump rally.</p><p>The Chicago Teachers Union <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2026/03/12/chicago-teachers-union-plans-to-join-may-1-no-school-no-work-day-of-action-wants-classes-canceled" target="_blank" ><u>wanted the school district to cancel classes</u></a> so that staff and students could participate in May Day, also known as International Workers' Day.</p><p>But CPS CEO Macquline King pushed back, saying she wanted to keep schools in session. The disagreement kicked off a <a class="Link" href="https://www.chalkbeat.org/chicago/2026/04/09/cps-will-be-open-may-1-during-protest-ceo-says-but-board-could-overrule-her/" target="_blank" ><u>firestorm of debate</u></a>.</p><p>On Thursday, they agreed to a compromise.</p><p>According to an agreement between CPS and the CTU, CPS will observe May 1, 2026 as a day of district-wide civic engagement.</p><p>To that end, 100 schools will be provided with buses and bag lunches so that students can attend an afternoon May Day rally. If the district can’t find enough buses, the city will pay for bus cards. </p><p>The district will also send out guidance to school leaders regarding the importance of civic engagement and suggest ways to involve students. </p><p>Also, the agreement says CPS cannot “retaliate against” staff who take the day off to participate in civic activities. </p><p>The agreement also releases 65 educators to go to Springfield to lobby for more state funds on May 13 and 27. Past CPS leaders have resisted releasing staff to do this kind of advocacy, even though the school district is in dire need of increased funding, largely due to the way the state funds schools.</p><p>The memo also designates May 1, 2028 as a teacher-directed professional day, meaning teachers could use it for civic action, and it creates a “May Day task force” between the district and CTU to “implement curriculum and school-based activities.”</p><p>Bridget Doherty Trebing, a teacher at Taft High School on the Northwest Side, said she thinks this compromise accomplishes CTU’s goal. She called the agreement “groundbreaking.”</p><p>“We have been asking all along to be in partnership with the district and it sounds like that is what has happened,” she said. “My students have been directly asking, when are more adults going to take a stand? They're paying attention to what's happening in the world. So this is a day that I think students are wanting to see.”</p><p>The CTU has been planning for a “no school, no work, no shopping” protest on May 1 for more than a year.</p><p>It’s long been a day to show support for labor unions and raise concerns about the government’s actions, but it's taken on new meaning this year as activists call for boycotting work to protest President Donald Trump’s mass deportation campaign and other policies enacted by his administration.</p><p>But for weeks, it was unclear who was calling the shots about May Day.</p><p>As recently as Thursday morning, Mayor Brandon Johnson <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/in-the-loop-with-sasha-ann-simons/2026/04/16/ask-the-mayor-april-2026-affordability-taxi-fares-new-zoning-chair-and-more" >said during an appearance on WBEZ</a> that he had directed his chief of staff and other city agencies to figure out logistics around the day. The CTU credited Johnson for helping come up with this compromise. </p><p>“If we’re going to transform our economies, if we’re gonna push for the ultra wealthy to pay their fair share in taxes, voices of working people, every day people have to rise up,” he told WBEZ.</p><p>The CTU thought King would go along with their plan after the school board took an informal vote in a closed session last week that showed the majority of members supported canceling school.</p><p>But even after that vote, King pushed back, saying she wanted an instructional day for students.</p><p>One Future Illinois, a super PAC that plans to be involved in the upcoming school board election, even commissioned a poll asking voters whether they thought CPS should be in session. Just over half opposed the idea of canceling school, while 40% said they were on board with school being canceled.</p><p>Gov. JB Pritzker also threw cold water on the idea of canceling classes for May Day when he was asked about it on Thursday.</p><p>“I think we need to make sure that our kids are getting every day of education that they deserve, and that parents demand,” Pritzker said. “Political operators trying to determine what the calendar should be for kids seems inappropriate.”</p>
entries.author: Emmanuel Camarillo

entries.title: Broadview reckons with spotlight it never wanted

spotlight it never wanted.</p><p>🗞️ <b>Plus</b>: Cabbies are conflicted about fare hikes, an ex-Gangster Disciple's debt, Chicago Public Schools' May Day decision and more news you need to href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-widuuly-l-d/" target="_blank" >Lauren FitzPatrick</a> and <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-widuuly-l-h/" target="_blank" >Nader Issa</a></h5><p><b>Blitzed community</b>: Leaders and residents of the small village of Broadview had no say in the scope or magnitude of President Donald Trump’s blitz that shuffled thousands of migrants through the U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement facility that has long operated in the quiet town. The word "Broadview" is now synonymous with the federal facility that became an immigration detention site and drew contentious protests.</p><p><b>Unexpected costs</b>: Just as the village had gotten its financial house in order, it was hit with nearly $400,000 in unexpected costs as a result of last fall’s campaign — about 10% of the small municipality’s discretionary budget. That’s in sharp contrast to local governments elsewhere in the country that have contracted with the feds to have their costs covered for detaining immigrants in local jails. Broadview Mayor Katrina Thompson recently made an ambitious request for federal reimbursement.</p><p><b>Sun-Times investigates</b>: We take a look at Broadview before the blitz, assess the impact after it and hear how the 7,900 people living in the unassuming western suburb are still digging out.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-widuuly-l-k/" target="_blank" ><b>READ Rezin/Sun-Times file</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-widuuly-l-u/" target="_blank" >CPS will be in session May 1 though rally field trip is scheduled</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-widuuly-l-o/" target="_blank" >Sarah Karp</a> and <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-widuuly-l-b/" target="_blank" >Emmanuel Camarillo</a></h5><p><b>Class in session</b>: Chicago Public Schools will be in session for a full day May 1, known as May Day, but it will also be an official civic day of action during which hundreds of students will be able to take a "field trip" to a massive pro-labor, anti-President Trump rally.</p><p><b>Key context</b>: The Chicago Teachers Union wanted the school district to cancel classes so that staff and students could participate in May Day, also called International Workers’ Day. But CPS CEO Macquline King pushed back, saying she wanted to keep schools in session. The disagreement kicked off a firestorm of debate.</p><p><b>Where things stand</b>: On Thursday, CPS and CTU agreed to a compromise. CPS will observe May 1, 2026, as a day of districtwide civic engagement. To that end, 100 schools will be provided with buses and bag lunches so that students can attend the afternoon May Day rally downtown.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-widuuly-l-n/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>CHICAGO GANGS ✶</h4><div class="Enhancement" No. 1 man says he’s drowning in debt from court-ordered fine</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-widuuly-l-x/" target="_blank" >Frank Main</a></h5><p><b>No money</b>: Gregory Shell was once the right-hand man to Gangster Disciples boss Larry Hoover in a $100 million-a-year drug empire, according to federal authorities. Now, he says he’s flat broke. And he’s asking a judge to wipe out most of what’s left of his court fine. "It’s unfair," Shell said. "The interest is more than my original fine, which was $15,000."</p><p><b>Key context</b>: Shell and Hoover both were sentenced in the 1990s to life in prison for running a drug conspiracy. They were shipped to the nation’s toughest prison, the Colorado "supermax." Last year, they were sprung after winning presidential commutations of their federal convictions. But their legal troubles aren’t over. Hoover, 75, is still serving a state life sentence for murder and is asking Gov JB Pritzker for executive clemency.</p><p><b>Financial burden</b>: Shell, 68, is back at home on the South Side, and back in court, trying to get out of most of the remaining debt from his federal case. Former President Joe Biden, in one of his last moves in office, pardoned Shell in January 2025 and shaved $10,000 off his fine. Prosecutors say Shell still owes $1,461 in principal — plus a whopping $20,630 in interest on that — and argue he shouldn’t be let off the hook.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-widuuly-l-c/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>PUBLIC SAFETY 🚔</h4><div class="Enhancement" Store Day returns with live bands, exclusive pressings and $1 deals</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-widuuly-l-il/" target="_blank" >Morgan Ciocca</a></h5><p>Record Store Day is an annual tradition celebrated around the country but gets special treatment in Chicago. On Saturday, expect more than three dozen shops in the area to offer live events and fresh pressings, including a 20th-anniversary release from a-Ha and famed private recordings of John Coltrane’s band by musician Frank Tiberi in the 1960s.</p><p>Chicago’s independent record stores know how to celebrate, often drawing lines around the block — some even starting Friday night. Here are some shops offering perks just for Record Store Day.</p><p><b>Beverly Records, 11612 Western Ave.</b><br>The cast of The Goodman Theatre’s "Ma Rainey’s Black Bottom" will kick off the day with a ceremonial needle drop of a collection of Rainey’s blues hits and a raffle of tickets for the show.</p><p><b>Dusty Groove, 1120 N. Ashland Ave.</b><br>Limited releases, free gifts with every purchase, doughnut holes, coffee and a morning magic show.</p><p><b>Out of the Past Records, 4407 W. Madison St.</b><br>Exclusive Record Store Day titles, plus a selection of $1 used vinyl.</p><p><b>Pinwheel Records, 1459 W. 18th St.</b><br>See live music from Macie Stewart, Flowering and Mail. Plus there will be pastries from chef Becky Carson of Cerdito Muerto and a sidewalk sale.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-widuuly-l-ir/" target="_blank" ><b>MORE STORES HERE</b></a></p><hr><p> </p><h4>LET’S HEAR FROM YOU 🗣️</h4><p><b>What's the first vinyl album you ever bought, and from where?</b> </p><p><a class="Link" href="mailto:newsletters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" ><i>Reply</i></a><i> with your answer (please include your first and last name). entries.author: Matt Moore

entries.title: Man sitting in car killed in South Deering shooting
entries.summary: <p>A man sitting in a car was killed in a shooting Friday morning in South Deering on the South Side. </p><p>The man, 44, was in the car around 10:50 a.m. in the 1600 block of East 95th Street when he was shot multiple times in the body, Chicago police said. </p><p>He was taken to the University of Chicago Medical Center where he was pronounced dead, police said. </p><p>Area 2 detectives were investigating. </p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Officer Krystal Rivera’s partner hid after fatally shooting her and failed to provide aid, new video shows
entries.summary: <p>Body camera footage released Friday shows that Chicago Police Officer Carlos Baker sat in a stairwell for more than 90 seconds after shooting his partner Krystal Rivera last summer, leaving her bleeding from a gunshot he initially said had been fired by someone else.</p><p>Videos show the officers chasing a man into an apartment building. Baker then booted open an apartment door and encountered a second man, who appears to aim a gun at the officers on videos released by the city’s Civilian Office of Police Accountability.</p><p>Baker turns, appears to trip over himself and fires the gunshot that hit Rivera, then runs up a stairwell and calls for help, the videos show. When Baker comes back down the stairs, he first steps over Rivera’s lifeless body before bringing her down the stairwell.</p><p>The video footage doesn’t show Baker rendering aid to Rivera.</p><div class="VideoEnhancement Enhancement" data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="video-de0000" name="video-de0000"></a> <div class="VideoEnhancement-player"><bsp-youtubeplayer data-video-player class="YouTubeVideoPlayer" data-player-id="fa4bb29e30dc24a6d9cccc91c1bef72e0" data-video-id="pC592whQ6Ww" data-video-title="Body-worn camera footage of Officer Carlos Baker from when his partner, Krystal Rivera, was killed" > <iframe id="YouTubeVideoPlayer-fa4bb29e30dc24a6d9cccc91c1bef72e0" allowfullscreen src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/pC592whQ6Ww?enablejsapi=1"></iframe> </bsp-youtubeplayer> </div> </div> <p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2025/06/05/chicago-police-officer-shot-cpd-crime-shooting" target="_blank" >Rivera’s death</a> was the first fatal officer-on-officer shooting in Chicago in nearly 40 years, and the first since the widespread adoption of body-worn cameras in the 2010s.</p><p>COPA released only two videos related to Rivera’s death and didn’t say how many it withheld, a significant departure from past practice and apparently at odds with city policy requiring the release of videos and reports when an officer shoots someone.</p><p>In the shooting death of Adam Toledo, the agency released 23 body-worn camera video files. In the shooting death of Dexter Reed, the agency released 17 of those files, including video of officers who arrived after the shooting. In the most recent video release by COPA, from January, the agency released nine videos, including seven the agency tagged as occurring after the shooting. </p><h3><b>Rivera’s family says partner ‘left her to die’</b></h3><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-130000" daughter " 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/53876b6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5761x3233+0+305/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F3a%2Fd8%2F408c63e444f588e0a9735ddcfa7d%2Friverashooting-070325-10.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ba0619a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5761x3233+0+305/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F3a%2Fd8%2F408c63e444f588e0a9735ddcfa7d%2Friverashooting-070325-10.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Yolanda Rivera, mother of Chicago Police Officer Krystal Rivera, is comforted by attorney Antonio Romanucci (left) and Rivera’s stepfather, Rico Thompson, after calling for an independent investigation into her daughter’s death in July 2025. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Rivera’s mother <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/12/10/krystal-rivera-carlos-baker-chicago-police-department-wrongful-death-lawsuit" target="_blank" >filed a wrongful death lawsuit</a> last year alleging Baker had been struggling to accept her daughter's decision to end their romantic relationship when he fatally shot her during the foot chase.</p><p>The lawsuit, filed in Cook County court against Baker and the Chicago Police Department, states that the breakup happened after Rivera learned that Baker was living with another woman while dating Rivera. Rivera threatened to tell his live-in girlfriend about their relationship.</p><p>The lawsuit alleges Baker showed up uninvited at Rivera’s home the day before Baker fatally shot Rivera.</p><p>After the shooting, Baker then “ran in the opposite direction and left her to die”, according to the lawsuit, which states he failed to provide medical aid, call for an ambulance or acknowledge he was the shooter.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-6d0000" name="image-6d0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e72a198/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8e%2F36%2F0109ae2e8c536907fe9f8c44dde2%2Fmerlin-101780167.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f65cc27/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8e%2F36%2F0109ae2e8c536907fe9f8c44dde2%2Fmerlin-101780167.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ae1d218/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8e%2F36%2F0109ae2e8c536907fe9f8c44dde2%2Fmerlin-101780167.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Newly graduated police officer Krystal Rivera talks about why she decided to become an officer after a graduation ceremony, Wednesday, Oct. 20, 2021 at Navy Pier. She said she wanted to become a police officer because of the opportunity to help others." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ae1d218/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8e%2F36%2F0109ae2e8c536907fe9f8c44dde2%2Fmerlin-101780167.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9f7f3c8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8e%2F36%2F0109ae2e8c536907fe9f8c44dde2%2Fmerlin-101780167.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ae1d218/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8e%2F36%2F0109ae2e8c536907fe9f8c44dde2%2Fmerlin-101780167.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Officer Krystal Rivera talks to the media after graduating from the Chicago Police Academy in October 2021. Rivera was shot and killed by her partner, Carlos Baker, during a foot pursuit in June 2025.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p> John Catanzara, president of the Fraternal Order of Police union, defended Baker's conduct after the body camera video was released Friday.</p><p>"He reacted better in that situation than I want to say 90% of officers would, because it was that chaotic and tragic of a … scene," said Catanzara. "Many officers would have ran down the … stairs possibly right away ... and subjecting themselves to then get shot.</p><p>“And then you'd have two shot officers on the staircase if the offenders were still in the apartment — which he had no idea that they had bailed at that point."</p><p>Authorities arrested and charged one man, accused of pointing a gun toward Baker once he was in the apartment, within a couple days of the shooting. A second man, the one who Baker and Rivera had chased into the apartment, was arrested and charged about two weeks later.</p><p>Their cases are pending, court records show.</p><p>About a week after the shooting, a judge <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2025/09/10/krystal-rivera-carlos-baker-chicago-police-shooting-judge-barbara-lynette-dawkins" target="_blank" >barred the release</a> of any material related to the two criminal cases or the administrative case opened against Baker after the shooting. The order prevented COPA from releasing videos within 60 days of the shooting, which is required by city ordinance.</p><p>The publisher of the Illinois Answers Project, the Chicago Sun-Times and other media organizations asked the judge to undo her secrecy order last summer. She declined, but an appeals court overturned her order in March as “an abuse of discretion.” COPA released videos and some files related to the shooting Friday.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-710000" name="image-710000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7c1cb0d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6881x3862+0+363/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F52%2Fc8%2F35b017cc4eb5857b3744715ce2ca%2Fcopshot-060725-6.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2cb3574/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6881x3862+0+363/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F52%2Fc8%2F35b017cc4eb5857b3744715ce2ca%2Fcopshot-060725-6.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/51db22e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6881x3862+0+363/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F52%2Fc8%2F35b017cc4eb5857b3744715ce2ca%2Fcopshot-060725-6.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="A building in the 8200 block of South Drexel Avenue on June 6, 2025 where Chicago Police Officer Krystal Rivera was fatally shot the night before. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/51db22e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6881x3862+0+363/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F52%2Fc8%2F35b017cc4eb5857b3744715ce2ca%2Fcopshot-060725-6.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/48ac4bd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6881x3862+0+363/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F52%2Fc8%2F35b017cc4eb5857b3744715ce2ca%2Fcopshot-060725-6.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/51db22e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6881x3862+0+363/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F52%2Fc8%2F35b017cc4eb5857b3744715ce2ca%2Fcopshot-060725-6.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Officer Krystal Rivera was shot and killed by her partner, Carlos Baker, after they chased a man into a building in the 8200 block of South Drexel Avenue in June 2025.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Pat Nabong/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><b>A checkered past</b></h3><p>Baker had <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/06/27/krystal-rivera-carlos-baker-chicago-police-department-gresham-district-police-discipline-copa-civilian-office-police-accountability" target="_blank" >racked up more than a dozen misconduct complaints</a> by the time authorities say he inadvertently shot and killed Rivera as they confronted the two armed men inside an apartment filled with guns and drugs.</p><p>Baker accrued five of those complaints as a probationary officer, when he could have been summarily fired because he had few union protections.</p><p>During that time, Baker was accused of flashing a gun at a woman he’d met online while she was on a date with another man at a North Side bar. The woman later refused to cooperate with investigators, and Baker faced no discipline, records show.</p><p>Baker’s record of complaints is unusual among Chicago police officers, especially for one so early in his career. Only 5% of Chicago police had six or more misconduct complaints from 2018 through 2023, according to data from the Invisible Institute.</p><p>Baker applied to the Gresham District’s tactical team in March 2024, but <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2026/01/15/chicago-police-department-carlos-baker-krystal-rivera-gresham-district-jon-hein" target="_blank" >Patrol Chief Jon Hein quashed the move</a>, citing Baker’s lengthy disciplinary history, according to an internal memo.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-580000" name="image-580000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/59a6671/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2400x1347+0+198/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2b%2F32%2F3a0bd2ee4093b469f6246be0b191%2F14df0e91-00ba-4b92-86fc-affa090b7b9f.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/212dcef/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2400x1347+0+198/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2b%2F32%2F3a0bd2ee4093b469f6246be0b191%2F14df0e91-00ba-4b92-86fc-affa090b7b9f.jpg"
