data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Chicago Police Officer Carlos Baker</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Chicago Police Department</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>After submitting a second application in January 2025, Baker won a spot on the tactical team, a coveted and competitive position that’s often used as a stepping stone for promotions.</p><p>Between the two applications, Baker ran into more trouble.</p><p>He failed to activate his lights or sirens as he chased a stolen car in June 2024 that ultimately went flying into the air and wrecked six other vehicles, records show. Baker then <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2026/02/19/chicago-police-department-krystal-rivera-carlos-baker-gresham-district-misconduct-discipline-investigation-cpd" target="_blank" >accidentally fired his Taser</a> while chasing the driver over a fence. He was docked two days of pay over the crash.</p><p>In between applications, he was also found to have been insubordinate and to have called his sergeant an expletive, records show. Baker had worked on the tactical team for a few months before shooting Rivera.</p><p>Each time Baker applied to the team, he had the backing of his district commander, Michael Tate, who worked under Supt. Larry Snelling in areas of the department where Snelling held top command positions earlier in his career.</p><p>Tate has declined to comment since the shooting, and the police department hasn’t answered questions about the shooting or Baker’s conduct. Tate was promoted late last year to street deputy, a high-ranking position responsible for responding to and commanding the scene at major events citywide.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2025/06/05/chicago-police-officer-shot-cpd-crime-shooting" target="_blank" >Chicago police officer was ‘unintentionally’ shot and killed by partner during Chatham foot pursuit</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/12/10/krystal-rivera-carlos-baker-chicago-police-department-wrongful-death-lawsuit" target="_blank" >Family of Chicago cop killed in botched chase sues Chicago Police Department and partner who shot her</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/06/27/krystal-rivera-carlos-baker-chicago-police-department-gresham-district-police-discipline-copa-civilian-office-police-accountability" target="_blank" >Chicago cop who inadvertently shot and killed his partner has a lengthy disciplinary record in a short career</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Sophie Sherry

entries.title: Marimar Martínez, a quien le disparó 5 veces un agente federal, testificará en audiencia en Washington
entries.summary: <p>Marimar Martínez, la mujer de Chicago que sobrevivió a cinco disparos de un agente de la Patrulla Fronteriza (CBP), testificará ante el Comité de Seguridad Nacional en Washington, D.C. la próxima semana y el comité de los demócratas también están pidiendo que el subdirector de personal de la Casa Blanca, Stephen Miller, y “el zar de la frontera”, Tom Homan, comparezcan.</p><p>La comparecencia de Martínez, Miller y Homan es voluntaria en la audiencia del 22 de abril y llegó a solicitud de los demócratas de la minoría en el comité y el presidente republicano Andrew Garbarino, republicano por Nueva York, se aseguró de incluir algunas críticas a los demócratas en cartas enviadas el miércoles.</p><p>"El Comité no considera que esta solicitud sea de buena fe y se utilizará, en cambio, como un intento de politizar la seguridad nacional y engañar a la opinión pública estadounidense", escribió.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-a50000" name="module-a50000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>El abogado de Martínez, Christopher Parente, confirmó el jueves su comparecencia en el segundo día de una audiencia destinada a discutir "los impactos perjudiciales del cierre del Departamento de Seguridad Nacional (DHS) en los estadounidenses".</p><p>Los republicanos del comité están utilizando la audiencia para culpar a los demócratas por el cierre que ya ha durado 61 días.</p><p>Garbarino también criticó a la miembro del comité, la representante Delia Ramírez, demócrata por Illinois, en las cartas, escribiendo que Ramírez el 10 de febrero "se negó a permitir que los testigos respondieran a alguna pregunta", y declaró, "DHS no se puede reformar; debe ser desmantelado".</p><p>La audiencia del 22 de abril marcará la primera vez que Martínez testificará ante un comité oficial. Martínez compareció por primera vez ante el Congreso el 3 de febrero en un foro público organizado en respuesta a las muertes de dos residentes de Minnesota a manos de oficiales federales de inmigración, diciendo a los legisladores que quería que se responsabilizara a los agentes federales y escuchar una disculpa.</p><p>También dijo que quería que la administración de Trump reconociera <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/02/04/marimar-martinez-baleada-por-agentes-de-migracion-se-pronuncia-contra-asesinatos" target="_blank" >que “no es una terrorista doméstica”</a>. Martínez también asistió a una audiencia del Comité Judicial del Senado el 3 de marzo, sentándose apenas cinco filas detrás de la entonces secretaria de Seguridad Nacional, Kristi Noem, mientras Noem era presionada por el senador estadounidense Richard Blumenthal, demócrata por Connecticut, a reconocer que el disparo a Martínez fue “incorrecto” durante una tensa audiencia.</p><p>A pesar de la cobertura exhaustiva del tiroteo de Martínez y <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2025/11/21/desestiman-el-caso-contra-mujer-a-la-que-le-disparo-agente-de-la-patrulla-fronteriza-en-brighton-park" target="_blank" >los cargos de obstrucción a la justicia que fueron posteriormente desestimados</a> en su contra, Noem afirmó repetidamente que no conocía el caso.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/03/04/durante-audience-kristi-noem-dijo-no-conocer-a-marimar-martinez-la-mujer-de-chicago-a-la-que-un-agente-de-ice-le-disparo" target="_blank" >“Señor, no conozco la situación ni el caso”, dijo Noem</a>. “Lo investigaré para asegurarme de que todos los procedimientos se hayan seguido correctamente”.</p><p>Noem fue destituida de su cargo dos días después. Hubo murmullos de insatisfacción por parte del presidente Donald Trump tras dos días consecutivos de testimonios de Noem ante los comités del Senado y la Cámara.</p><p>Ramírez le dijo al Chicago Sun-Times que ve el testimonio de Martínez como una oportunidad para que los miembros republicanos del comité miren a un ciudadano estadounidense a la cara "y tengan que luchar con lo que hicieron para fallarle".</p><p>"Creo que es realmente importante que ella venga y confronte a las personas que son responsables de permitir lo que le sucedió, y que use su poder para poder hablar por la cantidad de personas que han sido baleadas, arrestadas, secuestradas y golpeadas por esta administración", dijo Ramírez. "Creo que también es importante para nosotros, y para personas como yo que representan a Chicago, darle más espacio y tiempo para poder aclarar tantos de los temas sobre los que estoy segura que van a intentar cuestionarla o, ya saben, mentir sobre lo que sucedió".</p><p>La congresista de Illinois calificó las críticas republicanas en las cartas del comité como "ridículas".</p><p>"La mayoría quiere encontrar cualquier excusa que puedan para no tener que ser confrontados por lo que están haciendo, pero también por lo que son cómplices", aseguró Ramírez. "No quieren ver a Stephen Miller y Tom Homan comparecer ante el comité. En algunos casos, es porque saben lo mal que están las cosas".</p><p>Los demócratas del comité han intentado citar a Miller y Homan, pero han sido rechazados por los miembros del partido republicano. Ramírez dijo que seguirán presionando por sus comparecencias y también están solicitando que Markwayne Mullins, el nuevo jefe de DHS, comparezca.</p><p>"Él debería venir ante nuestro comité para asegurarnos de que está tomando en serio estas reformas de políticas", aseguró Ramírez. "Sería inteligente por parte de Garbarino traerlo ante el comité para demostrarnos que él no es Kristi Noem, y que toma su trabajo y su juramento en serio, especialmente si quiere financiamiento".</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Tina Sfondeles

entries.title: Chicago murals: Women's suffrage art that started in Chicago is growing across U.S.
entries.summary: <p>A series of women's suffrage murals that took root in Chicago is expanding across the United States. Three murals that pay tribute to the 1920 passage of the 19th Amendment, which gave women the right to vote, have gone up in the Loop, and the murals have been so well-received that the organizers behind them are planning additional murals commemorating women’s suffrage in New York City and Portland, Oregon, as part of the National Suffrage Mural Series.</p><p>“People just don’t know that there was a time when women didn’t have the right to vote. They didn’t know that it took 70-plus years and multiple generations of women. They also don’t know that the effort was multiracial,” says mural series co-director Michelle Duster, a Chicago-based author, historian and the great-granddaughter of journalist and early civil rights leader Ida B. Wells.</p><p>Initially branded the Chicago Suffrage Mural Series, two of the three original murals, which were intended for a site near Columbia College Chicago, were blocked when a parking lot owner refused to rent the artists space to stage their paint and scaffolding. He called the murals “too political,” according to a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2021/11/15/22783199/speak-up-mural-womens-suffrage-columbia-college" target="_blank" >2021 Sun-Times story</a>. Two years later, the murals found their permanent home about a block away.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-b30000" name="image-b30000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/29ea3db/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4770x2677+0+251/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F04%2Fbe%2F4b6862f04ee69641f989b939b151%2Fmurals-suffrage-260419-08.jpeg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7d90c01/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4770x2677+0+251/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F04%2Fbe%2F4b6862f04ee69641f989b939b151%2Fmurals-suffrage-260419-08.jpeg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7c2ba9a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4770x2677+0+251/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F04%2Fbe%2F4b6862f04ee69641f989b939b151%2Fmurals-suffrage-260419-08.jpeg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Two murals recognizing the women's suffrage movement appear on side of a building, one showing a yellow flower and one featuring the "... I'm speaking" phrase that Kamala Harris said during a vice presidential debate. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7c2ba9a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4770x2677+0+251/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F04%2Fbe%2F4b6862f04ee69641f989b939b151%2Fmurals-suffrage-260419-08.jpeg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1bd7c32/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4770x2677+0+251/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F04%2Fbe%2F4b6862f04ee69641f989b939b151%2Fmurals-suffrage-260419-08.jpeg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7c2ba9a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4770x2677+0+251/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F04%2Fbe%2F4b6862f04ee69641f989b939b151%2Fmurals-suffrage-260419-08.jpeg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>The “Votes for Women” and “Speak Up!” murals at 623 S. Wabash Ave. often appear as backdrops on social media posts.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Given the current political climate, the message is especially important, say Duster and co-director Neysa Page-Lieberman, who previously served as executive director of Exhibitions and Performance at Columbia College and chief curator of the Wabash Arts Corridor.</p><p>“Our rights continue to be stripped away, and it’s accelerated in the past few years," Page-Lieberman says. "We can’t just rest on our laurels and think because we had this hard-fought, hard-won victory 100 years ago that we will always have that.”</p><p>The Chicago murals sit along the Wabash Arts Corridor in the Loop. They are made up of:<br></p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" id="rte-b0024982-374f-11f1-9779-ab04fb0dfc67"><li>"On the Wings of Change" at 33 E. Ida B. Wells Drive by artist Jasmina<i> </i>Cazacu, who goes by the artist name Diosa. This portrait mural shows a teenage girl who seems to be dreaming of 10 Chicago suffragists, including Ida B. Wells. <a class="Link" href="https://library.colum.edu/archives/college-history/buildings/33-ida-b-wells-drive.php" target="_blank" >The building houses Columbia College offices, classrooms and the school’s radio station</a>.</li><li>"Speak Up!" at 623 S. Wabash Ave. by artist Dorian Sylvain. It features the popular quote that former Vice President Kamala Harris said in a debate with then-vice presidential candidate Mike Pence: “… I’m speaking.” <a class="Link" href="https://library.colum.edu/archives/college-history/buildings/623-south-wabash.php" target="_blank" >The building is owned by Columbia College and contains classrooms, offices, science labs, art studios and more</a>.</li><li>"Votes for Women,"<i> </i>also<i> </i>at 623 S. Wabash Ave. by artist AB Productions, includes a yellow rose, a symbol of suffragists, and commemorates 70 years of activism before women received the right to vote.</li></ul><div class="Enhancement" Wells Drive in the Loop." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5589b52/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5464x5464+1364+0/resize/300x300!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F47%2F4b%2Fc106e04d4f879c3df464619b33eb%2Fmurals-suffrage-260419-022.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0928b87/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5464x5464+1364+0/resize/600x600!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F47%2F4b%2Fc106e04d4f879c3df464619b33eb%2Fmurals-suffrage-260419-022.jpg 2x" width="300" height="300" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5589b52/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5464x5464+1364+0/resize/300x300!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F47%2F4b%2Fc106e04d4f879c3df464619b33eb%2Fmurals-suffrage-260419-022.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIzMDBweCIgd2lkdGg9IjMwMHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> </span> </div> </div> </div></div><div class="FlickityCustomNavigation-wrapper"> <div class="FlickityCustomNavigation below"> <button class="FlickityCustomNavigation-button-prev" type="button" aria-label="Previous"> <svg class="FlickityCustomNavigation-button-icon"><use xlink:href="#icon-left"></use></svg> </button> <button class="FlickityCustomNavigation-button-next" type="button" aria-label="Next"> <svg class="FlickityCustomNavigation-button-icon"><use xlink:href="#icon-right"></use></svg> </button> </div> </div></div> </bsp-carousel> </div> </div><p>Cazacu, who splits her time between Pilsen and Mexico City, said she modeled the girl in the portrait on a 14-year-old family friend. “She was four years away from having the right to vote in the next presidential election. That was a way for me to make it more personal.”</p><p>Sylvain, of Hyde Park, said the "Speak Up" mural holds meaning for her after growing up in a politically involved home and witnessing the social changes of the '60s, '70s and '80s.</p><p>“As much as I can take for granted growing up in the time that I did, I also can really see how we are sliding backwards and it’s really frightening,” she says. For example, Sylvain witnessed Roe v. Wade pass, granting women the right to an abortion in all states, and then be overturned.</p><p>Now, the murals are regularly included on walking tours of the Wabash Arts Corridor, Duster says. She's met students from Roosevelt and Loyola universities and Columbia College who view them for different class assignments. Occasionally, the murals show up as backdrops on social media. </p><p>"You never know when it’s going to pop up," Duster says. If someone seeks a Chicago cityscape photo with a feminist angle, "they'll want that in the background."</p><p>In New York City, Little Village artist Sam Kirk is planning the next installment of women's suffrage murals.</p><p>The suffrage movement is “something that we’re living through in many ways right now,” she says. “I’m hoping that I’m able to remind people of that — how much work has been done and why we need to keep a lot of this work in place.”</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-d00000" name="module-d00000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Murals and Mosaics Newsletter</div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-990000" name="html-embed-module-990000"></a><div class="piano-sidebar-newsletter-murals-and-mosaics"></div> </div> </div></div> </div><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-b20000" name="module-b20000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Chicago’s murals and mosaics sidebar</div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/murals-mosaics" target="_blank" >Chicago’s murals & mosaics</a></h3>Part of a series on public art in the city and suburbs. <b>Know of a mural or mosaic? Tell us where, and email a photo to </b><a class="Link" href="mailto:murals@suntimes.com" target="_blank" ><b><i>murals@suntimes.com</i></b></a><b>. </b>We might do a story on it.<br></div> </div><p></p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-d80000" name="html-embed-module-d80000"></a><div class="flourish-embed" data-src="visualisation/629878"><script src="https://public.flourish.studio/resources/embed.js"></script></div> </div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: Genevieve Bookwalter

entries.title: Barbara Flynn Currie, 'trailblazer who opened doors for generations of women' dies
entries.summary: <p>After a vote in the Illinois House on a key part of then-Mayor Rahm Emanuel’s pension relief plan in 2016, Barbara Flynn Currie did something not often seen in these times of divided, dysfunctional government. She crossed the aisle and shook hands with the three Republican lawmakers who broke ranks with the GOP and voted to override Gov. Bruce Rauner’s veto of a measure deferring police and fire pension payments.</p><p>That was Currie, 85, who died Thursday. She not only represented her Hyde Park district in Springfield for 40 years — 20 as majority leader and the first woman to hold that role in the Illinois General Assembly — she was a tireless promoter of active, engaged, effective government.</p><p>"Last night we lost a giant," House Speaker Emanuel "Chris" Welch, D-Hillside, posted on his Facebook page Friday. "Barbara Flynn Currie was more than a leader — she was a trailblazer who opened doors for generations of women in the Illinois House, many of whom continue her legacy today. ... She set the standard for what it means to serve with purpose. Her impact will be felt for generations."</p><p>Her district encompassed Hyde Park, Woodlawn, South Shore and Kenwood, and she was a vigorous proponent of liberal causes, such as prohibiting sexual harassment in the workplace, reforming school funding and offering all-day kindergarten. She spearheaded a compromise on welfare reform and helped extend state contracts to minority- and female-owned businesses.</p><p>In 2009, she chaired the special 21-member bipartisan committee that recommended the impeachment of Gov. Rod Blagojevich.</p><p>”We stand here today because of the perfidy of one man: Rod Blagojevich,” said Currie. “To overturn the results of an election is not something that should be undertaken lightly.”</p><p>Every member of the Illinois House and Senate, save one, voted to impeach.</p><p>With women making up a record 32% of state legislatures across the country, it might be difficult to remember the male world that Currie entered. When she was elected in 1978, fewer than 11% of Springfield lawmakers were women. When she announced her retirement in 2017, that figure was more than a third, and in 2025 the Illinois Legislature was 42% female. </p><p>Then-House Speaker Michael Madigan's decision to name her as majority leader in 1997 was unexpected: Downstate Democrats felt they had a hereditary right to the position, didn’t like the powerful post to pass to a Chicagoan, a woman, and perhaps worst of all, a liberal. Women across the spectrum saw it as a milestone.</p><p>”Republican women gave me flowers,” Currie later recalled. “Secretaries and staff in the Capitol were thrilled. One of my girlfriends nearly ran her car off the road. The depth of excitement was really quite thrilling.”</p><p>Still, some of Currie’s supporters looked askance at her playing ball with Madigan.</p><p>”To them, Currie was a sellout for taking the appointment from the hated machine politician Madigan,” Rich Miller of Capitol Fax wrote. “It never occurred to most of them that Currie’s new position would give their viewpoints an important new seat at the grown-ups table.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-ad0000" name="image-ad0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a54186a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+1406/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F94%2F7b%2F0451ff254f08aeb5aa1e1a7865f9%2Ffourth-cst-070514-10.JPG 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0aff75c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+1406/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F94%2F7b%2F0451ff254f08aeb5aa1e1a7865f9%2Ffourth-cst-070514-10.JPG 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/dc3cb7c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+1406/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F94%2F7b%2F0451ff254f08aeb5aa1e1a7865f9%2Ffourth-cst-070514-10.JPG" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="State Rep. Barbara Flynn Currie at the annual Hyde Park 4th of July parade. Friday, July 4, 2014 " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/dc3cb7c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+1406/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F94%2F7b%2F0451ff254f08aeb5aa1e1a7865f9%2Ffourth-cst-070514-10.JPG 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/63687b0/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+1406/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F94%2F7b%2F0451ff254f08aeb5aa1e1a7865f9%2Ffourth-cst-070514-10.JPG 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/dc3cb7c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+1406/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F94%2F7b%2F0451ff254f08aeb5aa1e1a7865f9%2Ffourth-cst-070514-10.JPG" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Then-state Rep. Barbara Flynn Currie at the annual Hyde Park Fourth of July parade in 2014. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Sun-Times file photo</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Among the causes she promoted were gun control and abolishing the death penalty.</p><p>Barbara Flynn was born in LaCrosse, Wisconsin, the daughter of Francis and Elsie Flynn. When she was 7, her family moved to the South Side, where she attended St. Thomas the Apostle Catholic School and the University of Chicago Lab High School. Her mother was a schoolteacher, and her father taught social work at the University of Chicago, where she studied before dropping out to marry David Park Currie in 1960. Eventually she returned and received her bachelor’s degree in 1968 and master’s degree in 1973, both in political science.</p><p>She worked as vice president for the Chicago League of Women Voters from 1965 to 1969. Later, she taught government at DePaul University and was an assistant study director at the National Opinion Research Center.</p><p>Currie was elected to the Illinois General Assembly's 24th District in 1978; her district changed to the 26th District in 1983 and the 25th in 1993.</p><p>“Barbara Currie has tackled many, many complex issues with a keen intellect, fairness and balance,” Madigan said when she <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2017/9/15/18357519/house-powerhouse-barbara-flynn-currie-not-seeking-re-election" >announced her retirement </a>in 2017, opting to not seek reelection in 2018.</p><p>Her husband, a revered legal scholar and teacher, died in 2007.</p><p></p><p>Survivors include two children, Stephen and Margaret, and four grandchildren.</p>
entries.author: Neil Steinberg

entries.title: Cubs getting back Matthew Boyd, lose Daniel Palencia, will be without Cade Horton longer
entries.summary: <p>Friday was a good-bad-ugly kind of day for the health of the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs" >Cubs’ </a>pitching staff.</p><p>Left-hander Matthew Boyd said he’s on target to return to the rotation Wednesday, the team put closer Daniel Palenica on the 15-day injured list and manager Craig Counsell said Cade Horton could miss 16 months after undergoing elbow surgery.</p><p>With a run of misfortune that has 10 pitchers on the injured list, the Cubs were due for some good news. They finally got it after Boyd, who has been out with a biceps strain, threw 3⅔ innings for Triple-A Iowa on Thursday.</p><p>“Things went good. I feel really good today,” said Boyd, who allowed three runs, four hits and a walk with six strikeouts in Columbus, Ohio. “That’s the important thing. Everything feels normal for the day after a pitch. Excited for Wednesday, and we’ll build toward that each day.”</p><p>Boyd last pitched April 1, when he threw 98 pitches in 5⅔ innings against the Angels and struck out 10. But the conditions — 39 degrees at first pitch with 21 mph winds — might have been a factor in the injury. He wasn’t wearing long sleeves.</p><p>“It had nothing to do within the outing,” Boyd said. “I’m sure the weather had something to do with it, [and] sitting for 20-plus minutes in cold weather. You’re throwing more reps down below because you’re trying to stay ready for that inning. There’s always different little dynamics that can change, all these different stressors that are not consistent from start to start.”</p><p>The Cubs had sent 10 men to the plate in a five-run third inning.</p><p>“I have over my career pitched through a lot of things, and you learn from those experiences,” Boyd said. “You’re not asking to be on the IL. It’s more, ‘Hey, this is how I’m feeling, let’s monitor it. … Sometimes it’s better to take a breather.”</p><p>Palencia will do just that after going on the IL with a left oblique strain, retroactive to Tuesday. The Cubs did not announce a corresponding move. Palencia last pitched Sunday, when he threw the ninth inning and earned the win in a 7-6, comeback victory against the Pirates. He has one save and hasn’t allowed a run in five innings this season.</p><p>Meanwhile, Horton’s star-crossed Cubs career took another turn with the news that he could miss 16 months after having surgery on his ulnar collateral ligament. Horton left his start April 3 after one inning, and the Cubs put him on the injured list with a right forearm strain. He had been expected to miss the rest of this season.</p><p>“When something like this happens, the only thing you can do is worry about what’s next,” Counsell said. “Putting that on some big timeframe isn’t very helpful. [With] Cade, we talked about just worry about today and make today the best you can and keep doing that. That’s how you somehow speed this process along. If you get too far ahead of yourself, that doesn’t help.”</p><p>Elsewhere, Counsell said reliever Phil Maton (right knee tendinitis) will throw a bullpen session Saturday.</p><p>“It’s an important bullpen,” Counsell said. “We’ve reached the stage where this is the next test. We don’t know how he’s gonna feel off the mound. Can he drive off the mound the way he wants to?”</p><p></p><p></p><p></p><p></p><p></p><bsp-listcarousel class="PageListE" data-module data-column-count=3 data-hide-authors="true" entries.author: Jeff Agrest

entries.title: Munetaka Murakami adjusting to MLB pitching along with anemic White Sox offense
entries.summary: <p>WEST SACRAMENTO, Calif. — After a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/04/01/munetaka-murakami-chicago-white-sox-debut" target="_blank" ><u>boisterous introduction</u></a> to Major League Baseball with three home runs in his first three games, the hits haven’t been easy to come by for Munetaka Murakami.</p><p>The slugger dubbed the Babe Ruth of Higo, Japan, has looked more like the Adam Dunn of yore with his three-true-outcome propensity, a big swing-and-miss profile the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" target="_blank" ><u>White Sox</u></a> knew and embraced when it brought him down to their price range for a two-year, $34 million deal.</p><p>But to revive one of baseball’s worst offenses, the Sox will need some more positive outcomes from Murakami beyond the strikeouts, walks and dingers their 26-year-old first baseman has piled up in the early going. Murakami says he knows he can get there as he adjusts in kind to the big-league pitchers adjusting to his prodigious bat.</p><p>“The pitchers have come out with a different variety of pitches obviously from the first series and now. But again, it’s about getting used to it,” Murakami said <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/04/14/noah-schultz-white-sox-mlb-debut" target="_blank" ><u>earlier this week</u></a> via interpreter Kenzo Yagi, after smacking his first homer in 10 days to break a 1-for-25 skid at the plate. “I’m seeing a lot of pitches right now. Getting used to it and getting that preparation is my first priority.”</p><p>As the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/04/16/white-sox-head-west-with-that-familiar-losing-feeling-rays" >Sox headed west</a> to open a three-game series against the Athletics, Murakami carried a lopsided .167/.346/.417 line over his first 19 career games, with five homers and five singles in 60 at-bats against a whopping 26 strikeouts and 17 walks.</p><p>He’s still buoying a cellar-dwelling Sox offense that was collectively below the Mendoza line (.195) to go with another MLB-worst OPS of .602.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-ea0001" name="image-ea0001"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/eed1be2/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3107x1744+0+0/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F20%2F9e%2Fb537fc5e42fbbdff093810711f9f%2F2271388937.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/31f6dcc/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3107x1744+0+0/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F20%2F9e%2Fb537fc5e42fbbdff093810711f9f%2F2271388937.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d7ec5df/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3107x1744+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F20%2F9e%2Fb537fc5e42fbbdff093810711f9f%2F2271388937.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Munetaka Murakami takes batting practice earlier this week at Rate Field." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d7ec5df/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3107x1744+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F20%2F9e%2Fb537fc5e42fbbdff093810711f9f%2F2271388937.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/27d4ba8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3107x1744+0+0/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F20%2F9e%2Fb537fc5e42fbbdff093810711f9f%2F2271388937.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d7ec5df/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3107x1744+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F20%2F9e%2Fb537fc5e42fbbdff093810711f9f%2F2271388937.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Munetaka Murakami takes batting practice earlier this week at Rate Field.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Michael Reaves/Getty Images</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>While Murakami has shown elite figures through mid-April in bat speed (74.1 mph), average exit velocity (93.7 mph) and barrel percentage (22.9%), his whiff (41.3%) and strikeout (33.3%) rates were among the highest in MLB, according to Baseball Savant.</p><p>And while opponents’ initial scouting reports had them going after Murakami with high heat, they’ve swiftly zeroed in on a tendency to chase offspeed and breaking pitches — more than half of which Murakami has waved on.</p><p>But he says he’s fine-tuning his own response by putting in time with the Sox’ Trajekt machine, an artificial intelligence-powered system that can replicate any pitcher for batting practice.</p><p>“I was kind of letting the easy pitches pass by me, the breaking balls in early stages. But now I’m seeing the ball a lot better and I’m really getting into the plate with a lot more confidence,” Murakami said. “The routine really hasn’t changed that much. Obviously if a pitcher that is new is coming up, I will go in Trajekt and see a lot of pitches from them.”</p><p>Sox hitting director Ryan Fuller said he expects Murakami to “dial it back a little bit, see the ball a little bit deeper, use the whole field.”</p><p>“His ability to make adjustments at a really high level has been very impressive and you can see it. His swings are starting to be a little bit more fluid, finding the barrel a little bit more,” Fuller said.</p><p>General manager Chris Getz said he’s not worried about his big offseason splash.</p><p>“He is going through an adjustment period with catching up to some fastballs,” Getz said. “We know what he's capable of doing. The great thing is he's already shown [it] and I know that he believes in himself. Getting back into a home routine I think is going to serve him well."</p><p>The Sox’ Trajekt stays at Rate Field, but a little more time to settle into his new city and an entirely new country could serve Murakami well, too. He got to take in his first Blackhawks game in a box at the United Center with his teammates on Monday, his first Chicago off-day.</p><p>“It was really exciting and fun,” Murakami said. “It’s a grind, I’m going home, hotel, facilities and then a routine of that daily. I really haven’t gotten into the fun part of enjoying Chicago yet, but it will come.”</p><p></p><p></p><p></p><div class="PageListL" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" entries.title: Sky sign Jordan Hobbs, Sidney Cooks to training camp contracts
entries.summary: <p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky" target="_blank" >Sky</a> signed guard Jordan Hobbs and forward Sidney Cooks to training camp contracts. Hobbs was drafted No. 34 out of Michigan in the 2025 WNBA Draft, though she did not make the Storm's final roster. She played with the Italian club San Martino this offseason, averaging 12.6 points on 38.1% shooting from three-point range.</p><p>Cooks is a 6-foot-4 big who played most recently for the Chinese club Shaanxi Red Wolves. She averaged 16.7 points and 7.3 rebounds for the Red Wolves. In college, she played for three different teams: Michigan State, Mississippi State and Seton Hall. She averaged 11.9 points, 5.5 rebounds and shot 35.6% from three.</p><p>The Sky’s roster now stands at 19 players. They can have 18 players at training camp at any given time. Sky center Elizabeth Williams is still playing overseas in the EuroLeague semifinals, which opens up an extra spot. She'd need to report to camp by May 1 per the WNBA's prioritization rule, which applies to players with three or more years of service.</p><p>Training camp begins April 19 <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky/2026/04/16/sky-to-practice-at-uics-flames-athletic-center-ending-the-sachs-era" >at UIC’s Flames Athletic Center.</a> </p><bsp-list-loadmore entries.title: Garret Anderson, Angels career hits leader who led team to World Series crown, dies at 53
entries.summary: <p>ANAHEIM, Calif. — Garret Anderson, the multitalented outfielder who became the Los Angeles Angels' career hits leader and led the team to its only World Series title, has died. He was 53.</p><p>The Angels announced Anderson's death Friday morning without immediately disclosing the cause or location.</p><p>Anderson reached the majors with the then-California Angels in 1994 and played for the club until 2008, primarily as a left fielder. He was a fixture in the heart of their batting order for his entire tenure, becoming the franchise's career leader in games played (2,013), hits (2,368), RBIs (1,292), total bases (3,743), extra-base hits (796), doubles (489) and grand slams (8).</p><p>"The Angels organization is mourning the loss of one of our franchise's most beloved icons, Garret Anderson," Angels owner Arte Moreno said in a statement. "Garret was a cornerstone of our organization throughout his 15 seasons, and his stoic presence in the outfield and our clubhouse elevated the Angels into an era of continued success, highlighted by the 2002 World Series championship. Garret will forever hold a special place in the hearts of Angels fans for his professionalism, class and loyalty throughout his career and beyond. His admiration and respect for the game was immeasurable."</p><p>The Angels will wear a memorial patch this season bearing Anderson's initials, the team announced. They will hold a moment of silence Friday before the Angels host the San Diego Padres.</p><p>Anderson was a three-time AL All-Star who finished as high as fourth in the AL MVP balloting, and he won two Silver Slugger awards. He memorably won the Home Run Derby and the All-Star Game MVP award in 2003 at what is now Rate Field. </p><p>His 272 career homers are third in Angels history behind Mike Trout and Tim Salmon. Only Trout has scored more runs in Angels history than Anderson.</p><p>In 2002, Anderson batted .306 and drove in a team-leading 123 runs for the then-Anaheim Angels, who won 99 games and earned a wild-card playoff berth. The Halos stormed through the playoffs to this franchise's only championship, overcoming a 3-2 series deficit to Barry Bonds and the San Francisco Giants to win the World Series.</p><p>Anderson was a key factor in the Fall Classic, batting 9 of 32 with six RBIs. He drove in the final three runs of the series with a tiebreaking three-run double in the third inning of the Angels' 4-1 victory over the Giants in Game 7.</p><p>Anderson finished his career with Atlanta and the Los Angeles Dodgers before his retirement in 2011. He was inducted into the Angels' Hall of Fame in 2016, and he had regularly worked for the team as a television broadcaster on its pregame and postgame shows over the ensuing decade.</p><p>Anderson was born in Los Angeles on June 30, 1972. He attended Granada Hills High School in the suburban San Fernando Valley before the Angels drafted him in the fourth round in 1990.</p><p>The Angels said Anderson is survived by his wife, Teresa, daughters Brianne and Bailey, and son Garret "Trey" Anderson III.<br></p>
entries.author: AP

entries.title: Administración Federal de Aviación ordena reducir vuelos de verano en O'Hare, citando seguridad y eficiencia
entries.summary: <p>Citando preocupaciones de seguridad y eficiencia, la Administración Federal de Aviación (FAA) anunció que reducirá el volumen diario de vuelos en el Aeropuerto Internacional O'Hare este verano en más de 300 vuelos.</p><p>La reducción se produce mientras las aerolíneas habían planeado un aumento en los vuelos para este verano en comparación con el año pasado. Esto incluye a las dos aerolíneas más grandes del aeropuerto, United Airlines y American Airlines, que han estado luchando por espacio en las puertas en O'Hare, el aeropuerto más ocupado del país.</p><p>La FAA limitará los vuelos diarios en O'Hare a 2,708 desde el 17 de mayo hasta el 24 de octubre.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-500000" name="module-500000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>Esto es una disminución de los 3,080 vuelos diarios que las aerolíneas habían planeado para este verano.</p><p>El secretario de Transporte, Sean Duffy, dijo que el horario original de verano era "poco realista" y habría "excedido drásticamente" lo que la infraestructura del aeropuerto y los controladores de tráfico aéreo podían manejar. El horario inicial de verano de 2026 habría sido un aumento del 15% en los días de mayor tráfico en comparación con el verano pasado, agregó la FAA.</p><p>“Nuestra prioridad es la seguridad del público que vuela, y eso significa garantizar que los horarios de las aerolíneas reflejen lo que el sistema puede manejar de manera segura”, declaró el administrador de la FAA, Bryan Bedford, en un comunicado.</p><p>Según la FAA, menos del 60% de los vuelos en O'Hare fueron puntuales el verano pasado.</p><p>La FAA trabajó con las aerolíneas y funcionarios de O'Hare para llegar a un compromiso entre reducir las operaciones y satisfacer las necesidades de las aerolíneas.</p><p>United Airlines dijo que la reducción es una “solución que tiene sentido para todos los que se preocupan por el éxito de O'Hare”.</p><p>Un portavoz de American Airlines dijo que la compañía está “contenta de haber asegurado un nivel suficiente de vuelos a través del proceso de la FAA para operar un centro de conexiones exitoso en O'Hare este verano”.</p><p>“Agradecemos al secretario Duffy, al administrador Bedford y a sus equipos de liderazgo por actuar con rapidez para asegurar que los habitantes de Chicago y todos los consumidores continúen beneficiándose de una competencia razonable y para ayudar a minimizar las interrupciones de vuelos durante la ajetreada temporada de verano”, dijo American Airlines.</p><p>Un portavoz del Departamento de Aviación de Chicago calificó la orden de la FAA como un “enfoque reflexivo” para asegurar que las limitaciones no se extiendan más allá del verano y que las operaciones de vuelo no sean menores que el año pasado.</p><p>El año pasado, la FAA apuntó a O'Hare para recortes de vuelos durante un cierre del gobierno. Esto llevó a una escasez de controladores y a decenas de cancelaciones de vuelos diarios.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Kade Heather

entries.title: Illinois House revives school cellphone ban; bill awaits scrutiny in Senate
entries.summary: <p>The Illinois House resurrected a push to ban cellphones and other wireless devices in classrooms during school hours, advancing legislation to to the Senate late Thursday. </p><p>The change would allow Illinois to join numerous states that have enacted similar laws.</p><p>Passed with bipartisan support, the ban would take effect at the start of the 2027-28 school year, and includes tablets, personal laptop computers, gaming devices and wearable devices, like smart watches and Meta glasses. The legislation applies to public and charter schools and leaves room for them to implement policies crafted by their school boards. </p><p>Whether devices are banned during lunch or in the halls would be left up to the discretion of the school. Teachers could also authorize device usage if it's for a lesson.</p><p>Exemptions are carved out for English language learners, students who take care of a family member, and individuals with an Individualized Education Plan or a medical exemption.</p><p>“The research is clear,” said Rep. Michelle Mussman, a Democrat from Schaumburg, while testifying in support of the bill. “This constant source of distraction is lowering their academic progress, increasing anxiety, depression and cyber-bullying and inhibiting face to face communication at a critical time when youth are developing their social skills.”</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2025/02/21/chicago-students-share-mixed-feelings-on-pritzker-proposal-to-restrict-classroom-use-of-cellphones" >Chicago students share mixed feelings on Pritzker proposal to restrict classroom use of cellphones</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>The proposal was first brought forward by Gov. JB Pritzker last spring and passed out of the Senate with unanimous support. However, the House did not take a vote before the session concluded. After making a minor amendment to the legislation in the House, it now heads back to the Senate. </p><p>“Every parent, educator and caregiver knows the damage unchecked screen time and social media can do to our children,” a spokesperson from the governor’s office said in a statement. “We look forward to the Illinois General Assembly finishing the job and passing these vital protections for our kids.”</p><p>A poll released by Pew Research Centers in 2024 showed that 72% of high school teachers say phone distractions are a major problem; 33% of middle school teachers and 6% of elementary school teachers said the same. Of the other states that have enacted similar bans, most have generally reported success.</p><p>While not opposed to the bill, the Illinois Federation of Teachers raised concerns over the state implementing another school mandate without providing additional funding that could be used for containers or wall hanging pockets that store students' phones during class.</p><p>Few other states have included additional resources to help fund their new policies. New York allocated $13.5 million for schools that need assistance in purchasing storage solutions, and some states like Delaware and Pennsylvania have launched grant programs to help finance schools in need.</p><p>Some opponents have <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2025/02/21/chicago-students-share-mixed-feelings-on-pritzker-proposal-to-restrict-classroom-use-of-cellphones" target="_blank" ><u>expressed safety concerns</u></a> about the inability of students to have their phones with them during an emergency. While the bill states devices are not banned during emergencies, students would not have devices on their person in those moments. </p><p>Several school districts across the state already implement their own bans. Those policies would be permitted to remain under the bill, should it become law.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2025/02/28/ban-cellphones-illinois-schools-governor-jb-pritzker-readers" >Should Illinois adopt Gov. JB Pritzker’s proposal to ban cellphones in schools? Here’s what readers say</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Matt Trunfio

entries.title: Ford City Mall on the verge of shuttering after owner fails to make repairs
entries.summary: <p>What was once a popular shopping destination on the Southwest Side appears to be nearing closure.</p><p>Ford City Mall, which opened in 1965, could be forced to shut down after its owner, Namdar Realty Group, failed to make repairs to the deteriorating building, including fixing a malfunctioning fire suppression system and preventing constant flooding.</p><p>Cook County Circuit Judge Leonard Murray gave tenants seven days to present a plan to either close their businesses or vacate the building, following a court-ordered shutdown during a hearing Thursday. The next hearing is scheduled for April 23. </p><p>“This is about Namdar not taking responsibility,” Ald. Derrick Curtis (18th), whose ward includes Ford City Mall, said. “The city’s been telling them to come into compliance for years now. It’s become a public safety issue.”</p><p>Namdar Realty Group and the city's Department of Law didn't respond to a request for comment.</p><p>The city filed an emergency motion on April 10, months after real estate company Kurv Industrial, formerly Bridge Industrial, unveiled <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/real-estate/2025/07/16/ford-city-mall-losing-shoppers-bridge-industrial-evolution" target="_blank" ><u>plans to demolish the mall</u></a> at 7601 S. Cicero Ave. and redevelop the site into a $150 million industrial campus.</p><p>The nearly 60-acre property would be cleared to make way for four buildings designated for warehousing or light manufacturing, though no recent updates have been provided on the proposal.</p><p>Curtis said Namdar had the opportunity to revitalize the property and create a space where the community could shop and gather. Instead, he said, the company has built a reputation for purchasing distressed properties and “draining everything out.”</p><p>“I don't think it's fair,” Curtis said.</p><p>Aside from the issues inside the building, the mostly-deserted mall has been an attraction for drivers to drift their cars on the parking lot. Curtis said Namdar has refused to hire security to keep them away.</p><p>“The drifters come over and burn the tires and drift in the parking lot, make donuts and burn tires,” he said. “It's really been a bad situation with this particular company.</p><p>Namdar bought the mall in 2019 for $16.6 million, a far cry from earlier transactions. Real estate tycoon <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/business/2023/5/18/23728642/sam-zell-dies-obituary-billionaire-chicago-equity-group" target="_blank" ><u>Sam Zell</u></a> had the property for 25 years, paying a reported $75 million in 1987. Zell handed it to his lender in 2012.</p><p>During Thursday’s hearing, Murray denied a request from a JCPenney representative to have 30 days to respond to the motion. JCPenney is the only department store in the mall.</p><p>Curtis said tenants are concerned about what will happen to their leases if the judge decides to shut down the building. Among the building’s tenants are AMC Theatres, Foot Locker and Marshalls.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a entries.author: Cindy Hernandez

entries.title: Philz Coffee will hang Pride flags back up after taking them down
entries.summary: <p>After facing intense backlash for removing all Pride flags from its locations, Philz Coffee reversed course Friday and said the flags will go back up.</p><p>“Our Pride flags are staying up.” CEO Mahesh Sadarangani said in a Friday press release. “If a Pride flag came down, it can be put back up.”</p><p>“I made a mistake, and I am sincerely sorry,” Sadarangani said in the release. “To our Team Members, to our customers, and to the LGBTQIA+ community that has been with us since the very beginning, the confusion and hurt we caused around our new policy for Pride flags failed you.”</p><p>“I’m glad that they listened to everybody, and they made a change," one employee at a Chicago-area Philz told the Sun-Times. "I don’t think it should have happened in the first place.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>An artwork displaying a rainbow is on display at a Philz Coffee in Old Town, Friday, April 17, 2026. It is unclear whether the artwork was placed before or after the company announced that it planned on removing the ban on Pride flags.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Pat Nabong/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The employee, who requested anonymity out of fear for her job, said that Pride flags are hung in other storefronts all around the Philz location where she works. She said it was “strange” that the coffee shop was removing its flag.</p><p>“It’s definitely a part of this community," the employee said. “They’re everywhere."</p><p>Philz has historically been supportive of the LGBTQ+ community. In Friday's release, it said it will continue to donate to the same LGBTQIA+ organizations as it did the year before.</p><p>One of the Chicago-area locations, which once hung a Pride flag, said it will mount its flag on the empty hooks soon.</p><p>The Bay-Area based coffee company has six locations in the Chicago area — in Lincoln Park, Old Town, River North, Wicker Park and Hyde Park and Evanston.<br></p>
entries.author: Zoe Singer

entries.title: Restoration starts on pergola at North Lawndale’s historic Sears garden
entries.summary: <p>A faded Mediterranean Revival-styled pergola that is the main feature of a North Lawndale garden, created 120 years ago by Sears, Roebuck & Co., is now being restored.</p><p>Work began Friday on the 100-foot long pergola at 3330 W. Arthington St. The structure is the centerpiece of the nearly 2-acre garden built in 1907 as a rest spot for employees at what was then Sears' sprawling campus headquarters.</p><p>"People are going to be amazed by the work," the Rev. Reshorna Fitzpatrick, chairperson of Friends of Sears Sunken Garden's board, said. "The work is going to happen pretty fast, and it's actually going to be pretty beautiful."</p><p>The $1 million restoration is being funded by a grant from the Richard H. Driehaus Foundation.</p><p>The project is the first step in a larger effort to <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/opinion/2023/10/7/23904516/sears-garden-restoration-driehaus-grant-north-lawndale-historic-preservation-lee-bey-column" target="_blank" ><u>revive the entire garden</u></a>, an idea launched by the North Lawndale Community Coordinating Council’s Greening, Open Space, Water, Soil and Sustainability committee in 2021.</p><p>The completed project promises to bring new life and attention to one of the city's best small public green spaces.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-5c0000" name="image-5c0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" class="Figure-credit"><p>Manuel Martinez/WBEZ</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>It's also part of what has been a turnabout for this portion of North Lawndale since Sears left its campus for the Sears Tower a half-century ago.</p><p>Most of the landmark Sears buildings are now being reused, although its big administration building at <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2024/05/07/three-chicago-buildings-among-states-most-at-risk-historic-locations" >3333 W. Arthington St.</a> sits vacant.</p><p>Meanwhile, Sears no longer owns its Downtown tower, having decamped the skyscraper in the 1990s for a big new corporate campus in Hoffman Estates — that was demolished in 2024 for a data center.</p><p>The Sears Sunken Garden pergola is an off-white open-air structure composed of 20 classically-designed concrete columns set below a red clay tile roof.</p><p>An interior footpath runs through the length of the pergola. There are wood porticos with Doric columns at each end of the structure.</p><p>The restoration starts with workers taking paint samples from the structure as a prelude to hunting down historically correct colors, followed by concrete patching.</p><p>"This will take a few days. Then, it's a few months of heavy lifting" that includes replacing the pergola's roof tiles and wood trusses, while repairing its concrete columns, the project's principal in charge, architect Andrea Terry of Arda Design, said.</p><p>"I think the weakest links are places where the birds and the raccoons have made little holes so there's water getting in," she said. "We need it to be sturdy and have staying power, but to still retain the architecture and the features that were meant to be. So it will have less holes in it. It will look cleaner and brighter."</p><p>Berglund Construction and Terra Engineering are also working on the project.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A worker on a lift inspects the trellis and west portico of the pergola.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Manuel Martinez/WBEZ</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Fitzpatrick said the Friends of Sears Sunken Garden group is looking to raise at least another $4.5 million to remake the green space surrounding the pergola.</p><p>A plan by renowned Dutch designer <a class="Link" href="https://oudolf.com/" target="_blank" >Piet Oudolf</a> — a creator of Millennium Park’s Lurie Garden — and Chicago landscape architect Chris Gent would give the garden new plantings, accessible paths and a reflecting pool.</p><p>The garden would be less Victorian than the one created by Sears' campus architects Nimmons & Fellows more than a century ago for company employees to eat lunch, catch a few warm breezes or listen to live music.</p><p>The revived garden would be "contemporary, naturalistic — less decorative than in the past," Oudolf told me in 2023.</p><p>Weather permitting, the pergola work should be completed this summer, Fitzpatrick said.</p><p>"It must have been so peaceful," she said of the garden's early years.</p><p>"You come and you sit down and enjoy the beautiful sun and this beautiful space," Fitzpatrick said. "How refreshing that must have been. We want to create that same experience for the families, people in the city and the people who come to tour the space."</p>
entries.author: Lee Bey

entries.title: Man, 21, critically wounded in Washington Park shooting
entries.summary: <p>A 21-year-old man is in critical condition after being shot Friday in the Washington Park neighborhood.</p><p>He was in a car in a parking lot about noon in the 6200 block of South Michigan Avenue when someone in another car pulled up next to him. Someone else got out of the other car and fired shots, Chicago police said.</p><p>The man suffered gunshot wounds to the chest and right shoulder, police said. He was taken in critical condition to University of Chicago Medical Center.</p><p>A woman at the scene told the Sun-Times she had earlier picked up her 18-year-old son from school when someone in a white Kia followed her home and into the parking lot of her apartment building at 6253 S. Michigan Ave.</p><p>The woman, who did not want to be named, said the Kia blocked her into a parking spot. Shortly after, someone else jumped out and opened fire.</p><p>“I’m just glad my son wasn’t hurt,” she said. She also was not injured.</p><p>The suspects then jumped back in the Kia and drove off, she said.</p><p>No arrests were reported.</p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Family of Adam Toledo drops lawsuit against city and Chicago police officer — but plans to refile
entries.summary: <p>The family of Adam Toledo has dropped their lawsuit against the Chicago police officer who fatally shot the 13-year-old boy, just as the trial got underway. </p><p>Opening statements in the wrongful death suit were expected to start later this month, but on Friday, the family’s lawyer filed a motion to voluntarily dismiss the case. Their lawyer, Adeena Weiss, said the move allows them to “continue pursuing the case in a manner that fully protects the rights of Adam Toledo’s family,” and refile within the next year.</p><p>“Let me be clear: this case is not over,” Weiss said Friday. “We will refile and continue seeking full accountability for what happened to Adam. Our focus remains where it has always been — on justice for this child and his family.”</p><p>Attorneys for the city said they will "defend the allegations when and if that happens." </p><p>Toledo was fatally shot during a foot chase over five years ago in Little Village. Chicago police officer Eric Stillman followed Toledo into an alley and fired a single shot at him, just seconds after the boy dropped a handgun and raised his hands. Toledo’s family filed a lawsuit shortly after against the city and Stillman.</p><p>The Civilian Office of Police Accountability <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2022/10/20/23415368/adam-toledo-eric-stillman-police-board-cpd-misconduct" target="_blank" ><u>recommended Stillman be discharged from the department</u></a>, finding he “acted inconsistently” with department foot-pursuit training policy and committed an improper use of deadly force. Then Chicago police Supt. David Brown disagreed, instead recommending Stillman be suspended up to five days for failing to activate his body-worn camera.</p><p>A disciplinary case has since stalled due to a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/police-reform/2025/09/16/chicago-police-union-fop-misconduct-discipline" target="_blank" ><u>years-long legal battle between the city and Chicago’s largest police union</u></a> over the handling of misconduct cases.</p><p>Stillman's attorney, Tim Grace, saw Friday's dismissal as an acknowledgment that the officer's use of force was "within policy and reasonable." </p><p>"He [Stillman] looks forward to moving on from this unfortunate incident," Grace said. </p>
entries.author: Sophie Sherry

entries.title: Arthur Loevy, Chicago lawyer who worked police misconduct, wrongful conviction cases with his son, dead at 87
entries.summary: <p>Arthur Loevy, a lawyer and labor leader who came out of retirement to work with his son at Loevy & Loevy, a powerhouse in wrongful convictions and police misconduct litigation, died April 11 after suffering a brain bleed in his sleep, his family said. He was 87.</p><p>The firm is nationally recognized for winning multimillion-dollar jury verdicts and settlements, often fighting the city of Chicago's Law Department.</p><p>Mr. Loevy was a master negotiator who honed his skills for decades as a leader with the Amalgamated Clothing and Textile Workers Union, fighting for worker's rights in tension-filled bargaining sessions. </p><p>He also previously worked for the Cook County Police Association, a group that helped suburban police departments unionize and negotiate contracts.</p><p>In 1998, he came out of retirement to practice law at a firm opened by his son, Jon Loevy. </p><p>Mr. Loevy was humble, but his experience quickly became an asset, according to his son.</p><p>"He started at the bottom, with 30-year-olds bossing him around, but we quickly realized he knew more than all of us combined and was the best negotiator we'd ever met, and he became the heart and soul of the place," Jon Loevy said. "He had spent his career negotiating union contracts against the toughest guys you could ever imagine — so he came in pretty well versed, and it was like watching an NBA player against junior varsity most of the time."</p><p>(Loevy & Loevy represents the Sun-Times in Freedom of Information Act cases.)</p><p>Mr. Loevy worked out of a conference room at the firm's West Loop office. Younger attorneys regularly stopped in to pick his brain.</p><p>"He could come up with a strategy and could get to the bottom of a matter in like 10 seconds," said attorney Matt Topic, who learned how to be confident at the negotiating table from watching Mr. Loevy. "When the other side got agitated, he would remain calm ... and it's a total flex. It shows your confidence and strengthens your position."</p><p>"He is the only person I've ever seen who could stare down and shout down a federal judge during a settlement conference," said attorney Steve Greenberg.</p><p>"He was always behind the scenes, but all the success we've had we owe to him and we're grateful," Jon Loevy said.</p><p>Mr. Loevy was born March 21, 1939, to Carl and Rose Loevy and grew up on the North Side. His father worked at a furniture store and his mother was a homemaker. Mr. Loevy attended Von Steuben High School, Carleton College and the University of Michigan Law School.</p><p>"He got out of law school could have done anything and decided to get involved in the union movement and make sure people were treated and paid fairly," Jon Loevy said.</p><p>"He was a big deal in the movement," he said, adding that he was recently looking at old photos of his father with Jesse Jackson, Ted Kennedy and Richard M. Daley.</p><p>In recent years, Mr. Loevy carried a leather briefcase and wore a jacket and a tie to a largely informal law office.</p><p>"He never got a cellphone and didn't use email to communicate, so he never got distracted," his son said. "He came to work the day before he died, as he did every day of the week, and contributed just as he always did and was absolutely 100% sharp, had not slipped an inch. And he died in his sleep and went out on top of his game. He could not have scripted it better."</p><p>Mr. Loevy, who most recently lived in a Gold Coast condo, made a habit of taking his watch off and giving to his son before closing arguments at a trial.</p><p>"Since he wasn't very sentimental, that was as close to sentimental as we ever got," Jon Loevy said.</p><p>Mr. Loevy also served on the executive board of the Chicago Federation of Labor, as a board member for Amalgamated Bank and as a trustee of the Textile Workers Pension Fund.</p><p>Mr. Loevy, who raised his family in Northbrook, also is survived by is wife, Barbette Loevy, his daughters Karen Horowitz and Debra Loevy, and nine grandchildren. Services have been held.</p>
entries.author: Mitch Dudek

entries.title: Chicago brilla con cortometrajes sobre discapacidades, encontrando comunidad en el Festival de Cine Latino
entries.summary: <p>Los creativos de la zona están apostando por la narrativa local a través de documentales cortos e historias que celebran las diferencias y la comunidad en la edición 42º del Festival de Cine Latino de Chicago.</p><p>El festival de cine de este año, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/04/13/el-festival-de-cine-latino-de-chicago-lleva-a-los-cinefilos-a-mexico-venezuela-cuba-y-mas-alla" target="_blank" >que comienza hoy jueves,</a> presenta cuatro películas producidas localmente, incluyendo un corto de la organización juvenil sin fines de lucro Free Spirit Media, así como una película que resultó de una colaboración entre Deep Focus Entertainment y Mass Epiphany Studios.</p><p>En total, el festival destaca 51 largometrajes y 31 cortometrajes de Latinoamérica, el Caribe, Europa y Estados Unidos. Todas las proyecciones, incluyendo las funciones de la noche de apertura y clausura, se llevan a cabo en Lake View, en el Landmark’s Century Centre Cinema, 2828 N. Clark St.</p><p>Estos son cuatro cortometrajes locales en el Festival de Cine Latino de Chicago de este año.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>“Abel” es un cortometraje de 20 minutos sobre la vida de Abel Berumen, un fotógrafo cuya vida cambió el día que despertó ciego.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Brian Zahm</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><b>“Abel” </b></h3><p><b>Proyección:</b> junto a <a class="Link" href="https://latidofilms.com/films/deaf/" target="_blank" >“Sorda”</a> el 17 de abril a las 7:45 p.m. y el 26 de abril a las 3:15 p.m.</p><p>La vida de Abel Berumen cambió cuando despertó ciego debido a una rara condición genética llamada pseudoxantoma elástico. Esencialmente, la transmisión entre sus ojos y su cerebro se ve interrumpida por la enfermedad.</p><p>Años después de perder la visión, Berumen conoció al profesor de la Universidad DePaul, Brian Zahm, en una clase de cine experimental y se enteró de su ceguera y de su increíble carrera fotográfica.</p><p>Zahm le preguntó si podía acceder a los archivos de Berumen para hacer un documental sobre su vida, y así nació el corto de 20 minutos, “Abel”. Berumen estaba en sus 50 y tantos años cuando se levantó en medio de la noche para ir al baño y, de repente, dejó de ver. “Todo era más oscuro que la oscuridad”, dijo.</p><p>Durante décadas, Berumen trabajó como fotógrafo comercial de moda. Había viajado a Nueva York y Miami para docenas de sesiones fotográficas e incluso abrió un pequeño estudio en el edificio Flat Iron en el vecindario de Wicker Park para su trabajo personal.</p><p>Pero ser ciego significaba que ya no podía trabajar, y amigos y colegas comenzaron a desaparecer uno a uno. Berumen se sintió enojado y solo, excepto por un rayo de luz en su vida: su pareja de más de 30 años, Gia.</p><p>Desde entonces, ha podido ver y experimentar la vida de nuevas maneras. “La vida no se detiene, no termina”, dijo Berumen. “Cuando las circunstancias cambian en la salud de uno… será diferente, será difícil, pero la vida vale la pena y sigues adelante [incluso] cuando sigue siendo doloroso”.</p><p></p><h3><b>“Cake” </b></h3><p><b>Proyección: </b>junto a “¡Basta Mamá!” el 19 de abril a las 6:15 p.m. y el 21 de abril a las 8:00 p.m.</p><p>El cortometraje de Erick Juárez, <a class="Link" href="https://www.imdb.com/title/tt36286601/" target="_blank" >“Cake”</a>, tiene como protagonistas a Camila Banus como Rosy y al actor de Chicago, Benjamín Delgado, quien tenía cinco años en el momento de la filmación, como su hijo. El cortometraje es una colaboración entre las compañías locales Deep Focus Entertainment y Mass Epiphany Studios.</p><p>La película es un cortometraje de 17 minutos inspirado en la historia real del séptimo cumpleaños del director. Ese año, la madre de Juárez decidió que ya no haría pasteles para las fiestas de sus hijos. Ella (y todos los que la rodeaban) se cansaron de los pasteles quemados o poco cocidos, así que la solución lógica fue comprar uno.</p><p>El cortometraje, ambientado en el vecindario de Pilsen, sigue a Rosy y Alex a través de la ciudad mientras se dirigen a una panadería elegante para obtener el postre de último minuto en un esfuerzo por hacer que el cumpleaños del joven Alex sea el mejor.</p><p>Terminan en la auténtica Bittersweet Pastry Shop & Café en Belmont Avenue, donde el actor de “Chicago Fire”, Joe Minoso, interpreta el papel del panadero principal.</p><p>Él hace una excepción para la pareja madre-hijo en el día especial de Alex, aunque las cosas no ocurren exactamente como se planeaba.</p><p>“Es la historia de mi mamá y mía y de cómo nuestra comunidad se unió ese día”, dijo Juárez. Expresó su esperanza de que el cortometraje les recuerde a los espectadores la importancia de “la amabilidad que tenemos unos por otros”.</p><p>“Lo sabemos, solo necesitamos un pequeño recordatorio. Solo necesitamos verlo”, dijo Juárez.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-30d7ab" class="Figure-credit"><p>Proporcionada | Festival de Cine Latino de Chicago</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><b>“Created with Purpose” </b></h3><p><b>Proyección</b>: junto a “Comparsa” el 23 de abril a las 5:30 p.m. y el 24 de abril a las 5:15 p.m.</p><p>La cineasta Ashley Ephraim se asoció con Tamika Lecheé Morales, fundadora del Autism Hero Project, para contar una historia que comenzó a desarrollarse hace más de una década.</p><p>Cuando el hijo de Morales fue diagnosticado con autismo en 2015, ella se culpó y “pasó por todas las etapas del duelo”. También había un sentimiento de vergüenza asociado con el diagnóstico. “No se lo conté a quienes estaban más cerca de mí”, dijo.</p><p>Pero después de un tiempo, Morales comenzó a ver el diagnóstico de su hijo como algo positivo y quiso ayudar a crear un mundo aceptante e inclusivo para él.</p><p>Fundó el Autism Hero Project, que desde entonces ha proporcionado subvenciones médicas, entrenamiento en competencias para primeros respondedores y defensa legislativa. La organización contrató a Ephraim para trabajar como editora de video en las galas anuales del Autism Hero Project.</p><p>Eventualmente, la compañía de cine de Ephraim, BrightCap Entertainment, ayudó a realizar “Created with Purpose”.</p><p>El documental de 20 minutos se centra en personas autistas locales y cómo sus familias, vecinos y fuerzas del orden pueden ayudar a crear un mundo más equitativo para aquellos en el espectro.</p><p>“Queríamos redefinir cómo las personas ven el autismo sin borrar la realidad”, dijo Morales. “Porque la verdad es que el autismo no es solo una experiencia. Este documental refleja esa verdad y desafía los estigmas que dicen que son menos o están rotos”.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure
